responseSet.title: Discussion of 'The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of 'The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice'

Carlos Plata-Mújica

Carlos Plata-Mújica

Carlos Plata-Mújica
Carrera 9 No. 88–68
Bogotá, Colombia

Carlos Plata-Mújica  

When I was nominated to discuss the changes in the use of dreams in psychoanalytical practice, I felt it an honour and at the same time interpreted it as a requirement to bring to this dialogue a synthesis, however partial, of the views of some Latin-American psychoanalytical authors. To accomplish this, I discussed the papers of Harold Blum (this issue) and Masud Khan (this issue) at the Colombian Psychoanalytical Society, as well as a series of notes about dreams which covered technical and practical aspects. These discussions (Laverde, 1975) helped me to pinpoint some matters, suggested others and form the basis of this present outline.

Before referring to the above papers separately, I shall contemplate them as a whole. Each presents a mode of thought, certainly coherent and exemplary, within their different orientation—outstanding examples of a commonly occurring phenomenon within the theoretical developments of psychoanalysis, whose diverse orientations show an undoubted capacity for logical and formal development of theories which embrace almost the totality of psychoanalytical problems.

When we study the orientation of ego psychology (Hartmann 1964); (A. Freud 1970) or that of the Kleinian positions (Klein, 1948)(and Klein in Riviere, 1952) or that of Winnicott's (1958) object relations, etc., we may trace and understand the way each finds its origin in Freud's works and notice how each follows a development which has its own powerful logic. Moreover, from a certain perspective and at a certain distance, we can appreciate that some of the conceptual divergences that separate them are not really divergences at all, but are sometimes methodological mistakes, and at other times overvalued affective tendencies (Plata, 1964), (1965b); (Plata & Meluk, 1965a), (1974).

If this is the case, the question arises as to what is happening in the psychoanalytical theory, and as to whether the theoretical building of metapsychology is taking an unjustified primacy over clinical practice, pushing aside in this way its immediate relation with the psychoanalytical process and situation. For my part, after a continuous examination of many of these theories over several years, I have arrived at the conclusion that all of them represent excellent descriptions of clinical events; that they use diverse terminologies derived from metapsychological points of view, and that the happening of the analytical situation is intended to elaborate them conceptually, as if they were theories, specifically concerning psychological development (genetic or historic) of some aspects of the mental apparatus. Therefore, I wish to emphasize at this point that Blum and Khan respond, within their respective approaches to systematizations that may not be criticized nor are liable to examination from the clinical point of view, since in their presentations they aspire, so far as I can understand, to show another aspect: the internal logic of their theories.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Edgar L. Lipton on 'Psychoanalytic Child Development Research and the Practice of General Psychiatry'

P. Kuiper

P. Kuiper

P. C. Kuiper
Prinsengracht 716
Amsterdam

P. Kuiper  

It is my pleasure to comply with the request of the Programme Committee by making a few comments about Dr Lipton's methodical and lucid lecture (this issue). Since there are no points with which I fundamentally disagree I would like to formulate a few thoughts which arose while reading his discourse, particularly with reference to points 1 and 2.

The subject of maternal deprivation remains an important one. By and large there seems to be an increasing tendency for neurotic tension to be discharged either in addiction or in that which we commonly refer to as psychopathic behaviour; destructive aggression to be specific. People tend to take two directions when trying to escape inner conflicts and the corresponding painful emotions: acting out of tensions and addiction, one of the greatest and most difficult problems in modern clinical psychiatry.

Two personality traits visible in all individuals with a predisposition to these forms of escape are: low frustration-tolerance and poor impulse-regulation. We do know that a warm relationship between a mother and child is a necessary condition for the development of effective impulse-regulation and frustration-tolerance, although it is, of course, more complicated.

A lack of security and impossibility to identify with parents who have a more or less structured superego and ego ideal make it impossible for many children to learn to cope with disappointments, to bear the grief inherent in each frustration and what is probably more essential, to conquer the grandiose fantasies that so often serve as a solution to frustration yet simultaneously increase the child's vulnerability. Of course we are aware of social factors which can also play a role in this respect.

No discussion about the relationship between child development and general psychiatry would be complete without mentioning Dr Mahler's research, so aptly summed up by Dr Lipton.

Insight into these stages of development and their disturbances brings us closer to an answer to the question of the cause of psychosis, especially those forms of psychosis where no cerebral disturbances can be established. I would like to point out that difficulties with symbiosis, individuation and separation can have a more harmful effect when there is a hereditary disposition. Here I refer to the Gottesman and Shields research: in his closing remarks Meehl stresses the importance of environmental factors in this respect. Freud's concept of 'complemental series' is actually very up-to-date.

Psychoanalytic Sociology, an Essay on the Interpretation of Historical Data and the Phenomena of Collective Behaviour

By Fred Weinstein and Gerald M. Platt. Baltimore and London: Hopkins University Press. 1973. Pp. 122.

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines  

This concise and closely reasoned book is written by an historian and a sociologist who are well acquainted with the psychoanalytical literature, particularly recent American ego psychology. The work is directed towards the development of a theory of social change, based on the integration of psychoanalytical hypotheses and assumptions with those of normative sociology.

1Those analysts who work with groups and who are acquainted with some of the relevant contributions to social psychology will therefore find it easier going than those who work entirely with individuals. To the latter may I recommend two works by Robert Nesbitt, The Sociological Tradition and The Social Philosophers, both published by Heinemann Educational Books, as fascinating and authoritative guides which will help to orientate themselves in this field.363

[[RunningHead]]

The Annual of Psychoanalysis, Vol. II

Edited by Chicago Institute of Psychoanalysis. New York: International Universities Press. 1975. Pp. 420.

Kurt Schlesinger

Kurt Schlesinger

Kurt Schlesinger  

This volume is difficult to review as a single entity since it is a collection of 20 papers covering a wide range of topics which are organized under rubrics touching on psychoanalytic aspects of history, philosophy, social and developmental psychology, educational theory and clinical work.

The section on psychoanalytic history deals ineluctably with Freud in its three contributions; Freud as an expert witness, Freud's Rund briefe 1920–1924 with the 'committee, ' and Freud's dreams analysed. It is a commentary on our history that it is so much, still, after some four score years, the history of one man.

Gunther and Trosman present Freud as expert witness testifying on the problems of malingering, compensation and treatment of the war neuroses. The historical vignette is sensitively developed in all of its implications. Gunther discusses the ambiguity with which Freud has to tread the narrow path between being politic and at the same time not sacrificing the integrity of his understanding of the neurotic process. In the current unresolved questions regarding where the allegiance of the therapist lies between social and personal poles there are important points raised here.

Carl Schorske's paper attempts to show that Freud in discussing his own dreams, gave full scope to an apolitical, ahistoric interpretation 'of human experience in which politics could be reduced to an epiphenomenal manifestation of psychic forces'. He discusses these dreams emphasizing what he feels Freud has slighted as political and social determinants such as his Jewishness, Austrian anti-Semitism and his dashed liberal hopes. Freud's emphasis on the subjective oedipal aspects of the dreams is interpreted as a flight from political rebellion and the re-emergence of this rebellion in covert form lies in the attack that psychoanalysis presented to cherished established beliefs. While Schorske the historian makes it clear that historico-political forces cannot be derived from and reduced to by-products of the human psyche, I would add that neither can the human psyche be reduced to a product of social and historic forces. We are left with an ongoing problem of the manner in which these two, the psychological and social aspects, do interact.

[[RunningHead]]

Destruction by Automobile. Accident or Suicide?

Edited by Norman Tabachnik. Springfield, Ill.: Charles C. Thomas. 1975. Pp. 288.

Peter Shoenberg

Peter Shoenberg

Peter Shoenberg  

This book presents research carried out by a group of psychoanalysts and psychologists from the Los Angeles Suicide Prevention Centre and the Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute. Their intention was to evaluate the presence and significance of possible suicide-like factors in automobile accidents so as to apply that understanding to the prevention of car accidents. A team of psycho-analysts, (themselves evaluated for their own bias) interviewed three groups of hospitalized subjects matched for age and sex, comprising a group of severely suicidal subjects, a group of drivers injured as a result of one-car accidents and finally a control group of post-appendicectomy patients. Their interviews included a number of specific questions aimed at eliciting information of a psychodynamic nature so as to reveal whether certain predefined self-destructive modes of thinking, experiencing and behaving were present in the subjects from the different groups. Significant 'others' who had been closely involved with these subjects were interviewed by an independent team so as to assess the reliability of the information obtained by the psychoanalysts. The psychoanalysts' own tape-recorded interviews on the subjects were also independently assessed by two separate teams of lay raters and psychologists.

Contrary to their expectations, the authors found no clear evidence of significant self-destructive traits in the car accident subjects, except with respect to alcohol consumption. By contrast, all the self-destructive traits (one of which is defined by the authors as consisting of over-frequent visits to the doctor!) were significantly increased in the group of suicidal subjects. In one of the early chapters there is a helpful review of the psychoanalytic literature on accident-proneness and it is interesting that the evidence of this study runs counter to the majority of metapsychological theories about this subject which regard the accident-prone individual as the possessor of many self-destructive traits. Also of interest is the author's observation that the psychoanalysts' private theoretical biases were not altered by the findings of this study.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel on 'The Implications of Recent Advances in the Knowledge of Child Development for the Treatment of Adults'

J. Thiel and N. Treurniet

N. Treurniet

J. H. Thiel
AMSTERDAM
N. Treurniet
Leggeloo 12
Dwingeloo (Drenthe)

N. Treurniet  

The Chairman, León Grinberg, welcomed the audience to this panel discussion and stressed the importance of the subject. Recently it had aroused a good deal of interest but also doubt. The Panel would try to uncover the motives both for the interest and the doubt. León Grinberg then said a few words about the professional background of each of the panel members. He expressed how sorry he was that Hanna Segal and Victor Smirnoff could not attend the panel for health reasons. Fortunately they could be replaced by Mrs Pick and Dr Thiel. He then announced that every panel member would begin by giving a summary of the main views contained in his position statement. After that the members of the panel would have a spontaneous discussion which finally would be followed by a discussion from the floor.

Starting in alphabetical order the Chairman gave the floor to Charles Brenner. Brenner read his position statement (published in the second 1975 issue of the Newsletter of the President of the I.P.A.) some elements of which are reported here. To put the matter succinctly, Brenner stated that he does not believe that any substantial implication for the psychoanalytic treatment of adults is contained in the recent advances in knowledge about child development. The facts that are available to him are consonant with the view that both in severely and in less severely ill adult patients an absence of conflict over oedipal wishes is apparent rather than real and that the so-called pre-oedipal features are chiefly the result of defensively motivated displacements and regression. Whatever may be the developmental faults due to very early experiences and/or the child's constitutional endowment at birth, they are accessible to analysis in adult life only after they have been, as Miss Freud put it, worked over and drawn into internal conflicts. Every child in the oedipal phase reacts to his instinctual drive derivatives with the psychological capacities he has at that time. The individual hierarchy of dangers, the individual preference of defences, why the resulting compromise is what it is, depend on many factors which, among others, include the child's psychic constitution when he enters the oedipal period. Although this depends in large part on pre-oedipal influences, these influences, important as they are, do not determine the final result nor are they ordinarily accessible to analysis in adult life. It is for these reasons, Brenner believes, that, excellent as they are, the recent advances in the knowledge of child development during the first two years of life carry no substantial implications for the psychoanalytic treatment of adults. A scientific theory is never more than the best guess one can make at a given time to explain the available data. Old theories have to be altered or discarded when they fail to fit new facts. New facts in the case under discussion would be examples of situations arising in clinical practice that illustrate the advantage of applying either to one's understanding of a case or to one's technique some of the fascinating findings of analysts who are engaged in early child development.

[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud

Edited by Paul Roazen. Makers of Modern Social Science series. Englewood Cliffs, N.J.: Prentice Hall. 1973. Pp 186.

Frederick Wyatt

Frederick Wyatt

Frederick Wyatt  

Before the reviewer can return to loftier aims he is, quite rightly and for the sake of elementary justice, expected to establish the author's purpose. What is the aim of this volume on Freud as a 'Maker of Modern Social Science' and for whom is it written? Several of the papers which make up the book, criticize Freud's ideas and attitudes so severely that the reader will wonder how Freud ever became a 'Marker of Modern Social Science'. On the other hand, Marcuse, the author of one of the papers, criticizes those unsparingly who saw fit to modify some of Freud's basic ideas and create new schools of psychoanalysis. Fromm is among Marcuse's black sheep and, indeed, he contributes the article to Roazen's book which is most critical of Freud. Several of the papers, critical of, or accepting psychoanalysis, are more concerned with a kind of social metatheory of conditions leading to it than with the thing itself. Fromm, Riesman and Marcuse belong in this group and, in a somewhat different manner, Parsons. Erikson's paper, published in 1965, deals with the application of psychoanalysis to history. Brunner and Fromm focus on what they believe to be Freud's image of Man, Fromm's contribution being a section of his book The Crisis of Psychoanalysis, published in 1970. Both Fromm and Marcuse present the bemused reader with the illuminating spectacle of two vastly divergent appraisals of Freud—including the extent of his radicalism—both derived from the same Marxist premises. Marcuse's essay is a part of his book Eros and Civilization, published in 1966. Then there is Parsons' important article 'The Superego and the Theory of Social Systems', first published in 1952. Riesman's 'The Themes of Work and Play in the Structure of Freud's Thought', one of a quartet of papers on themes in Freud was published in 1950. Sapir's fine paper, 'Cultural Anthropology and Psychiatry' appeared in 1932, and H. D. Laswell's methodological classic, 'The Prolonged Insight Interview of Freud' is of 1938. Roazen's 'Freud and Woodrow Wilson' (a part of his book on Freud: Political and Social Thought, 1968) conclude the volume.

[[RunningHead]]

Alice Hermann 1895–1975

Tibor Rajka

Tibor Rajka

Tibor Rajka  

Not long ago someone (rather ill-mannered, I must say) to whom I was talking about my plans for the following year, broke into loud laughter. To have plans at seventy? Of course, the pen can drop from one's hand at any moment. There is nothing the matter with that, there will be people to pick it up. I am not very much afraid of the interruption (however, I should very much like the end of my ability to work to coincide with the end of life). All in all, I can look back with good feelings. Not that I have done much, but that saying about 'one brick added to the building' comes from the very bottom of my heart. I am aware of my limits: what is expressed by the concise English saying, 'to do one's best', has given me the moral foundation to avoid tormenting myself too much with the charge of 'not being sufficiently up to the mark'.

This is what Alice Hermann wrote in Pedagógiai Szemle (Paedagogical Review) in 1964, looking back on her life and activity. Her wish about the ending of her life was granted: her ability to work was brought to a close by death. On 16 August 1975 the inexhaustible life gave place to death almost without transition.

On 29 May the Hungarian Paedagogical Association warmly celebrated Alice Hermann's 80th birthday. Alice Hermann enjoyed what is given to a few people only: to see the results and the aims of her activity come to life. She was devoted to a task that demanded bravery, self-sacrifice and persistence: she wanted to transplant into institutions, and more than that—to extend to an institutional level and on a social scale all that she had mastered as a research psychologist, as a theoretically trained, and for a time practising, psychoanalyst: the idea and the internal call of education into humane adulthood.

Alice Hermann felt it to be her duty to demand of herself exceptionally high standards. It was this drive towards high standards that brought her close to her future husband, Imre Hermann, and which later united them for 53 years. She had made professional contact with Hermann in 1919, long before their marriage, and they began their common investigations, the result of which was outlined in a joint publication, 'Zur Entwicklungspsychologie des Umgehens mit Gegenständen' in 1923. 'Der Hebelprinzipversuch als Intelligenzprobe', which was published in 1926, was also a work written in collaboration. This paper was soon followed by another joint study in 1927, 'How Does a Small Child Feel and Think?' During this common activity Alice Hermann's attention turned more and more devotedly towards the developmental phenomena of the child's mentality and then, many years later (in 1959) a beautiful book appeared, written together, The First Ten Years.

responseSet.title: Robert C. Bak—1908–1974
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Robert C. Bak—1908–1974

Martin Stein

Martin Stein

Martin Stein  

It is still very difficult to write anything which acknowledges that Robert Bak has died. On countless occasions during the past few years we knew he had been appallingly close to death, saved only by emergency measures. He talked of his predicament objectively, without anger or self-pity. We knew that he was in constant danger, yet at the same time he displayed such an attachment to life, particularly the good things of life, that we found it impossible to accept that it must indeed come to an end—and soon.

Born and educated in Budapest, he remained very much the Hungarian, perhaps the epitome of what we who are not of the elect think that a Hungarian should be. A Romantic, whose vulnerability and idealism were scarcely hidden by a veneer of cynicism, he deceived none of his friends. He was deeply devoted to psychoanalysis, which he approached with the kind of enlightened scepticism Freud had very early commended in analysand and analyst alike. Bak took nothing for granted except the need to follow the analytic path wherever it led, always aware that the search for ultimate understanding could never be completed. He was a fine scholar and clinician, intrigued by theoretical advances but never satisfied with them, even if they were his own; his was an inquiring and perpetually restless intellect.

His other basic principle, demonstrated in every aspect of his life, was the importance of aesthetic quality. He was far more likely to be offended by gross lapses of taste or by mediocrity, for that matter, than by what he regarded as merely erroneous. He was in fact both tolerant and even intrigued by 'sinners', so long as they demonstrated some regard for the (aesthetic) proprieties. But his tolerance did not extend to behaviour which he regarded as exploiting or vulgarizing psychoanalytic theory and practice.

He was openly and unashamedly an 'elitist', an epithet which he invariably accepted as a compliment. On many occasions he expressed in public his belief that psychoanalytic education should be kept entirely in the hands of those who devoted the bulk of their time and energy to the field of psychoanalysis, and that only full-time analysts should engage in training analysis. This did not prevent him, however, from recommending that psychoanalytic education be offered to many more people than now, including those who might never practice—and perhaps never should, in his opinion.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Henry Edelheit on 'Complementarity as a Rule in Psychological Research'

Jean Guillaumin

Jean Guillaumin

Jean Guillaumin
23 Cours Franklin Roosevelt
69006 Lyon

Jean Guillaumin  

I am thankful to Dr Edelheit (this issue) for the vigorous position he has taken up on a difficult and rather abstract question, namely what kind of links we have to set up between psychoanalysis and neurophysiology. Nowadays psychoanalysts so often feel guilty about the advances they have made in their own field and look with envy at the research patterns of other sciences! In such a matter we have until now perhaps not gone further than Freud. Edelheit is right in pointing out that the founder of psychoanalysis could probably never completely give up his secret early wish to create one unitary science of the human mind, including both a neurological description of brain functioning, and a psychological one of the findings resulting from a clinical approach. And it is true that some world-renowned scientists in physics, medicine and neurology (such as Oppenheimer, Bohr and Hughlings Jackson) have initiated an epistemologically better attitude, one which has led Dr Edelheit to his conclusions. I agree with him (in spite of the now old-fashioned and somewhat irrelevant word 'parallelism' he uses in his paper) in admitting that psychoanalytic psychology and neurophysiology are interconnected only by a 'complementarity' principle, which does not suppose any inclusion, intersection, reciprocity or injection (to speak as modern mathematicians do) of the terms and concepts of one language into the other. No doubt we must clearly renounce a synthetic or a syncretic 'psychophysiology' that would pretend to be both a very physiological and a very psychoanalytical psychology.

In my opinion Dr Edelheit's conception has, however, to be worked over further and, to some extent, made more sophisticated, in order to reach not only an intellectual credibility (that it certainly has) but also a real power to change some deep-seated defences turned against the full development of our science in many researchers, psychoanalysts or not. To speak only of the analysts, some of them have what I should call a peculiarly stubborn inferiority complex towards physical and natural sciences. A few overcompensate for this 'complex' by a pseudo-biological vocabulary and argumentation intending to reinforce their self presentation as very 'objective' scientists. Some others, on the contrary, compensate for it by a quasi-poetical use of verbal seduction, tending, (in extreme cases, pleading overdetermination) to a kind of ludicrous disavowal of all real precision about what they mean. As a matter of fact, Edelheit's views, although they seem accessible on the whole, are somewhat too general and perhaps too philosophical to be applied without any confusion to the theoretical and practical situations we all deal with in psychoanalysis, and to reduce thoroughly the scientific identity crisis that keeps on troubling some of us.

responseSet.title: Three Essays Plus Seventy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Three Essays Plus Seventy

W. Meissner

W. Meissner

W. W. Meissner
6 Sumner Road
Cambridge, Mass. 02138

W. Meissner  

The year 1975 marks the 70th anniversary of the publication of one of Freud's most remarkable and original contributions to psychological understanding—the 'Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality' (1905). Probably no other of his works has had as significant an impact on the development of psychoanalytic theory, with the possible exception of the 'Interpretation of Dreams' (1900). Like the dream book, the 'Three Essays' have undergone numerous editions, in each of which Freud made significant modifications and additions to the text. In the present consideration we will limit our focus to the original edition published in 1905, since it contains the essentials of his theory of sexuality which has remained essentially unaltered.

The development of Freud's notion of sexuality has been carefully documented countless times and can be traced with considerable accuracy through the Fliess correspondence and related documents dated 1892–99 (Freud, 1950a). Apparently the elements of the theory of sexuality were already formulated to a certain degree in his early paper on anxiety neurosis (Freud, 1895) and in other documents dating from about that same time. Similarly, by the time of his writing of the 'Project' in 1895 (Freud, 1950b), Freud had evolved an explanation of hysteria based on the traumatic effects of sexual seduction in early childhood. His conviction of the importance of early sexual seduction dominated his thinking until 21 September 1897, when he finally announced to Fliess that he had begun to doubt his seduction theory (Freud, 1950a, letter 69). The realization that the sexual factor was not due to an actual historical seduction but rather represented the product of fantasy activity provided a significant and telling shift in Freud's approach to the understanding of mental phenomena. Freud's own self-analysis led shortly thereafter to an almost concurrent discovery of the Oedipus involvement which put his sexual theory on a relatively firm footing.

Despite the early hints, it was not until 1905 that the theory of sexuality and its related theory of instincts burst into full flower, with the nearly contemporaneous publication of three major contributions—the 'Three Essays', the book on jokes, and the case history of Dora. It is my intention here to review the theoretical status of the formulations on sexuality and the conceptual framework within which they took shape and then to suggest some of the theoretical shifts in perspective that have evolved during the course of the subsequent 70 years of psychoanalytic development. This may offer us some clear indication for the direction of current psychoanalytic thinking about problems of sexuality.

[[RunningHead]]

Address of Welcome, London Congress

A. Limentani

A. Limentani

A. Limentani
5 Upper Wimpole Street
London W1M 7TD

A. Limentani  

This is the fourth time that the honour of welcoming members of an International Psycho-Analytical Congress has fallen to a President of the British Psycho-Analytical Society. This in itself is not a record, but it is something of which we are extremely proud. So: welcome to London. It is a place with pride in its ability to combine an unchanging charm with a capacity for profound and carefully developed change.

We believe that our tradition forms an ideal background to this Congress which is devoted to a consideration of 'Changes in Psychoanalytical Practice and Experience' and the 'Theoretical, Technical and Social Implications' of these changes which, of course, cannot be wholly dissociated from the changes that are taking place in society at large.

The history of psychoanalytical societies inevitably reflects, to some extent, the climate of opinion of the countries in which they are rooted and the British Society is no exception. Quite apart from everything else, we in London do pride ourselves in being 'with it'. It was Ernest Jones who broke fresh ground in the field of female sexuality with his paper on that topic and one of his many famous errands took him to Vienna in the early 1930s for a special discussion on the subject.

We have arranged a small exhibition on Psychoanalysis in Britain and, although I do not wish to bore you with a history of the British Society, I should like to remind you that the small nucleus of British psychoanalysts included the names of Ernest Jones, Sylvia Payne, John Rickman, James and Edward Glover, James and Alix Strachey, Ella Sharpe, Joan Riviere, Adrian Stephens, (Virginia Woolf's brother), Marjorie Brierley, Susan Isaacs, William Gillespie, Clifford Scott and Donald Winnicott. They initiated the tradition which has, to this day, provided the basic tenets of our outlook towards the development of theory and technique in the practice of psychoanalysis and which has also influenced our training methods. It was the climate of opinion that they were able to create in the British Society, a climate which encouraged a tolerance of differing points of view, within a general acceptance of the basic assumptions of psychoanalytic technique, and which enabled the British Society to welcome and take to itself such distinguished colleagues from the European continent as (I am mentioning them in the chronological order in which they came here) Melanie Klein, Paula Heimann, Eva Rosenfeld, Professor Freud himself and his daughter Anna Freud, Willi and Hedwig Hoffer, Michael Balint and many others. Their coming has greatly enriched the intellectual life of our Society and continues to influence our outlook on teaching psychoanalysis.

A Discussion of the Paper by W. W. Meissner on 'Three Essays Plus Seventy'

Leão Cabernite

Leão Cabernite

Leão Cabernite
Rua Aires Saldanha, 66 ap. 701
Copacabana Z C–07
20, 000 Rio de Janeiro R. J.

Leão Cabernite  

Seventy years ago, in 1905, Freud published his 'Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality'. Five years previously he had published his book on dreams (Freud, 1900) which, in its seventh chapter, described the mind according to a model called topographical. This same topographical model, replaced 18 years later by a structural model (Freud, 1923), together with the ideas Freud then espoused about instincts and the mechanisms of libido repression and charge–discharge, exerted an influence on his concepts of sexuality, clearly tinted with biopsychological undertones.

Freud's studies especially emphasized the quantitative aspects of libido use, assigning to sexuality some characteristics and pressures of a bio-economic and object-oedipal nature.

The origin of the Freudian ideas on sexuality and its role in the genesis of neuroses and psychoses dates back to 1895.

His Letters to Fliess (Freud, 1950a) the 'Project' (Freud, 1950b) and his paper on neurasthenia and anxiety neurosis (Freud, 1895) were written at that time and they contain the major ingredients of his future theory of sexuality: childhood sexuality (the Oedipus complex), anxiety neurosis and early childhood sexual seduction (which he connected to the development of hysteria).

Meissner (this issue) has studied the influences of Freud's early thoughts put forth in the 'Project' (Freud, 1950b) on his concept of sexuality (Freud, 1905). Its three major assumptions are: (1) the topographical operational model (Conscious, Preconscious and Unconscious) retaining the causal determinism theories (derived from Helmholtz's physics) directed towards a biologic determinism; (2) the economic point of view of the libido, including the constancy principle of all mental operations which would ultimately account for the pleasure principle. The studies about countercathectic forces are derived from this and such forces are expressed in repressive terms of shame, disgust, horror and morality. The blockage caused by these countercathectic forces would cause a remanaging and rechannelling of libido, discharged then in the shape of neurotic symptoms; and (3) the physiological and even chemical terms of Freudian thoughts about libido, instincts and erotogenic zones. Anxiety was seen essentially as transformed libido.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ego Psychology: Theory and Practice

By Gertrude and Rubin Blanck. New York and London: Columbia University Press. 1974. Pp. 395.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg  

The first part of this book reviews contributions to psychoanalytic ego psychology, starting with a brief outline of the development of

362

Freud's thinking in this area, followed by succinct summaries of the contributions of Hartmann, Kris and Loewenstein, Spitz, Mahler, Jacobson, Kernberg and Kohut. The second applies these concepts to the carrying out of long-term, intensive, psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The book is geared, therefore, to psychoanalytically orientated psychotherapists as well as to psychoanalysts. The precise summaries of the contributions of the authors mentioned are formulated in ways which make them clearly understandable to the former. Indeed, for the psychiatric resident and other mental health professionals interested in recent developments of psychoanalytic theory, they constitute an excellent introduction to contemporary ego psychology. These summaries will also be very useful for the psychoanalytic candidate as an overview and as a general introduction to a more detailed study of the work of the various authors included. For example, I do not know of a better condensation of Margaret Mahler's work in only eight pages.

Gertrude and Rubin Blanck's orientation to psychoanalytic psychotherapy is a blend of the best traditions of advanced ego psychological approaches to psychoanalytic psychotherapy with a specific developmental model based upon Mahler's thinking. I agree with their emphasis on Mahler's contributions, and I wish they had been more explicit in stating their theoretical reasons for selecting Mahler's approach as their basic underlying theory for the section on technique. However, when they are dealing with clinical material, the authors give direct, explicit reasons for various types of intervention, and their psychotherapeutic approach is contemporary and sophisticated. They stress the need for a clear differentiation of psychoanalysis proper from psychoanalytic psychotherapy and the need for an integrative theory covering both, specifying some of the quantitative differences which, at a certain point, become qualitative differences separating the two.

[[RunningHead]]

Changes in the Theory and Practice of Psychoanalysis

David Liberman

David Liberman

David Liberman
Santa Fe 2829, 50
Buenos Aires, Argentina

David Liberman  

In order to answer the challenge contained in this subject, I shall divide my exposition into the following parts: (1) Freud's references in relation to the future prospects of psychoanalysis and his way of conceiving of the mental activity of patient and analyst. Telephone and chess as models; (2) Semiotics as an auxiliary discipline to introduce new ways of theorizing on the psychoanalytic process; (3) The 'quasi colleague' patient as forming the bulk of patients nowadays. Some technical implications and a brief clinical example; (4) Technical considerations in reference to the analysis of overt psychotic patients and the relevance of the communicational aspect in the patient's family network.

1.

In 'The Future Prospects of Psychoanalytic Therapy' Freud (1910) wrote that he had not come to the end of his resources as regards the neuroses and could soon hope for substantial improvement in the therapy. He wrote: 'This reinforcement will come, I think, from three directions: (1) from internal progress, (2) from increased authority, and (3) from the general effect of our work' (p. 141). In the same article he came to the conclusion that at that time, 1910, he considered the analyst's interventions in session as a decisive factor in the cure, and that both, patient and analyst, take active part in the treatment. In his 'Recommendations' on analytic technique Freud (1912) employed a metaphor which gains momentum if seen in the light of the spirit of the time. Considering the theory of communication, the psychoanalytic process turns the analyst into a peculiarly qualified decoder of the unconscious messages sent by the analysand in an inadvertent manner. In this paper he says:

He (the analyst) must turn his own unconscious like a receptive organ towards the transmitting unconscious of the patient. He must adjust himself to the patient as a telephone receiver is adjusted to the transmitting microphone. Just as the receiver converts back into sound waves the electric oscillations in the telephone line which were set up by sound waves, so the doctor's unconscious is able, from the derivatives of the unconscious which are communicated to him, to reconstruct that unconscious, which has determined the patient's free associations (p. 116).

A Discussion of the Paper by Anton O. Kris 'On Wanting Too Much: The "Exceptions" Revisited'

Harald Leupold-Löwenthal

Harald Leupold-Löwenthal

Harald Leupold-Löwenthal
Weihburggasse 22
1010 Vienna 1.

Harald Leupold-Löwenthal  

Dr Kris has presented us with a paper (this issue) with which I can agree in many respects, concerning its theoretical and technical propositions. He tries to apply modern knowledge and theorizing on narcissistic phenomena and syndromes to a paper written by Sigmund Freud in 1916. And this is where I find some difficulty in following him, because he interprets in an a-historic way statements on observations and descriptions of cases (and very sketchy descriptions they are) that were made about 60 years ago, without in the least being aware of what Reichenbach (1938) called the 'context of discovery'.

It is rather important to raise the question of method here and Dr Kris will forgive me if I use him as an example for a very common occurrence in psychoanalytic publications. We analysts seem to have a certain leaning towards 'revisiting' Freud's works and only rarely do we realize that what we are actually attempting is a sort of travelling in a time machine. Because in a real journey through time we would not expect to find the same customs and costumes as nowadays!

Klauber (1968) has pointed to the advantages of emphasizing the value of a historical orientation, which are greatest when psychoanalytic controversies are being considered. He recommends an interpretation which regards controversies among analysts as historical phenomena and not simply as technical disputes in a technical discipline. But this applies not only to controversies but also to the uses of concepts and constructs in our theory, to the diagnostic labels used as well as to the nosological considerations attempted. Thiel (1966) has shown the trends in respect of a psychoanalytic nosology: (1) analysts agree about 'nosology and its discontents'; (2) analysts also agree in wishing psychoanalytic data to be used in establishing nosological classifications; (3) they disagree, however, sometimes fundamentally, when they start elaborating their points of view.

There is no doubt that a revision of psychoanalytic theory and concepts can be more appropriately undertaken at present by means of results secured within the context of justification rather than that of discovery, but we must not forget that the latter plays an important role when we try to compare Freud's findings—60 years later—with our present ideas. In dealing with problems concerning the philosophy of science, history of science has become extremely important (see Lakatos, 1971); (and especially Kuhn, 1971). The analysis of particular historical situations in the development of sciences has revealed the questionable nature of strict methodological rules in science, but it has also shown the fertility of using historical perspectives. It must be realized that there is a great similarity between the most important form of explanations in the field of history and in psychoanalysis. Both employ genetic explanations; descriptions of chronological sequences of events to explain the origin or development of a certain singular event (Leupold-Löwenthal, 1975).

[[RunningHead]]

Explorations in Autism

By Donald Meltzer, John Bremner, Shirley Hoxter, Doreen Weddell and Iska Wittenberg. Scotland: Clunie Press. 1975. Pp. 250.

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle  

The authors of this book are modest in their claim, stressing that they have located problems rather than solved them (p. 5). But if their main theory can be substantiated—and a prima facie case for it has been made—I think the book will deserve to be called a breakthrough. For, as far as I know, it gives the first coherent theory of autism, which, till now, has been a very baffling as well as a heart-rending illness.

Their overall theory rests on (and incidentally gives further support to) several recent theories about early failures in development, such as Bion's on mothers with an insufficient capacity to tolerate their infants' projective identifications of distress and rage; Meltzer's on 'dismantling' as an earlier defence against pain than splitting, by which multisensual concepts of things that can be seen, felt, heard, tasted and smelt are dismantled into their unisensual elements; Esther Bick's on children who in early contact with their mother fail to internalize her holding function so that they do not form a 'psychic skin' to hold them together; and also on children who fail to form three-dimensional concepts of objects which can contain spaces, and are therefore unable to identify either projectively or introjectively. The alternative identification by a skin-to-skin contact she calls 'adhesive identification'.

It is clear, I think, that children who suffer from these defects in order to escape pain will fail in the very first steps in concept-building which must precede their construction of a world-model. And because of these initial failures what Melanie Klein called the 'primal splitting' between good and bad objects will fail too, and this, of course, will cause other failures, including a failure of the Oedipus Complex to develop in a recognizable form. In particular, such a child will fail to learn to speak or think in words and so will be without an essential element in his equipment for dealing with all these later hurdles in development, which indeed can only crop up after the autistic state has been dealt with—and then, because of the loss of time, perhaps never adequately. For example, the lalling period in which a normal child expands his vocabulary is likely to be over before he begins to have a vocabulary to expand.

The Psychoanalytic Treatment of an Hysterical Character with Special Emphasis on Problems of Aggression

Myron Eichler

Myron Eichler

Myron Eichler
Talpiot Mizrach 110/15
Jerusalem, Israel

Myron Eichler  

SUMMARY

This has been a detailed clinical presentation of the psychoanalytic treatment of an hysterical character. From the case history and the details of the analysis, I have attempted to crystallize a picture of the genetic development of the aggressive drive and to illustrate the role aggression plays in this character disorder. The implications for handling the technical problems in the analysis of the hysterical character have also been detailed in this paper.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of 'Freud and Female Sexuality'

Tufik Meluk

Tufik Meluk

Tufik Meluk
Carrera 7 No. 21–30
Apt. 203 Bogotá, Colombia

Tufik Meluk  

When one undertakes the task of presenting or of discussing a work which has been previously defined in its scope and subjected to certain norms and special circumstances, the first thing one must do is to clarify the central question. On this occasion the central question is female sexuality in the work of Sigmund Freud, its theories (concepts and developments) and its expositive process.

Burness Moore (this issue) refers explicitly to the central theme of the Dialogue and advises the reading of Strachey's note and of his own work dealing with frigidity (Moore, 1964) in which he has a valuable bibliography of previous contributors. Following the recommendations of the Programme Committee of this Congress he proposes the discussion of Freud's major concepts from the vantage point of recent advances in ego psychology, in direct observations of children, and in biology.

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel (this issue) concentrates on those of Freud's works which refer to the theme of female sexuality, clarifies certain points and works through others to their ultimate conclusion. She emphasizes especially 'sexual phallic monism', human bisexuality, the role of the mother in Freudian theory and also the role of countertransference in promoting or impeding the process that leads to knowledge about female sexuality.

Following the methodological perspectives proposed by Moore, I shall occupy myself with the biological, the observational and the psychological.

That the biological is an essential factor in the human being is accepted by every analyst, and this biological 'destiny' manifests itself in the anatomical.

The adjective 'feminine' qualifies the woman as the biological organism which, after pregnancy, is responsible for the process of gestation, parturition, lactation and certain aspects of child care. Men and women are endowed with different although corresponding genital organs with specific products: the ovum and the spermatozoon, each with different chromosomatic formulas, XX for the woman and XY for the man.

Naissance Du Psychanalyste. De Mesmer À Freud.

By L. Chertok and R. de Saussure. Paris: Payot. Coll. Sciences de l'Homme. 1973. Pp. 270.

Didier Anzieu

Didier Anzieu

Didier Anzieu  

This is not only a very useful and interesting book, it is primarily moving. As we know, one of the co-authors, Raymond de Saussure, who introduced psychoanalysis into French-speaking Switzerland and who was probably the first analyst to work in French, died in the autumn of 1971, shortly after the International Psycho-Analytical Congress in Vienna, which he attended despite his illness and where for the first time psychoanalysts from all over the world, including Anna Freud, congregated in their 'home town'.

But that is not really what is moving. It was not by chance that Raymond, the son of Ferdinand who founded linguistics, became a psychoanalyst.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presidential Address, London Congress

Serge Lebovici

Serge Lebovici

Serge Lebovici
3 Avenue du President Wilson
F75116 Paris

Serge Lebovici  

Ladies and Gentlemen, I declare open the 29th Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association.

An event has taken place in London which, it seems to me, has a symbolic value for our Congress. The Executive Council of your Association has held a meeting in the house where Miss Anna Freud, its Honorary President, had invited us. During our discussions we were able to see the room where Sigmund Freud used to work during his last days, his couch and the important items amongst his collection of objects d'art. The inspiration our colleagues found during this meeting was probably due in part to the circumstances and to the place where we were. This is a lesson for us all, if we remember that the British Psycho-Analytical Society, whose importance in the history of the psychoanalytical movement you are aware of, welcomed Freud and his family warmly at the time when events had forced them to flee from Austria.

I am therefore pleased that this 29th Congress taking place in London gives us the opportunity to meet here in great numbers. We can see at this meeting many colleagues whom we are accustomed to seeing at our Congresses, but also, happily, many psychoanalytic candidates—these latter are, of course, the future of our Association.

Unfortunately, this meeting is also a sad occasion on which our thoughts must go to those who have passed away since our 28th Congress, and whose memory will officially be commemorated on Wednesday morning (23 July) during our Business Meeting. However, I do not want to miss this opportunity to recall the memory of Walter Joffe who, as you know, was President of the British Psycho-Analytical Society at the time when he invited us, on its behalf, to meet here in London. He was also the Chairman of the Programme Committee, and his death was certainly one of the consequences of the exhaustion to which all his duties condemned him. It is to him that we owe the idea of a Congress the scientific sessions of which will take place throughout in the four official languages with simultaneous translation. As you know, Miss Pearl King has been kind enough to fill the position that Walter Joffe left empty. Thanks to her and to the Co-Chairmen of the Programme Committee, André Green and Edward Weinshel, the programme has taken its final form, with the scientific works which will be read and discussed shortly.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Ishak Ramzy and Howard Shevrin on 'The Nature of the Inference Process in Psychoanalytic Interpretation: A Critical Review of the Literature'

Ernesto Liendo

Ernesto Liendo

Ernesto Cesar Liendo
Quinta Chunel, Calle Chivacoa
San Román, Caracas 106, Venezuela

Ernesto Liendo  

I fully agree with the importance given by Drs Ramzy and Shevrin (this issue) to the need for rigorously systematizing a psychoanalytical methodology in order to give psychoanalytic theory the scientific structure which, based upon clinical observation, will enable it to formulate laws applicable to the psychic system and eventually to offer predictions susceptible of proof.

I shall therefore make general comments on their paper while leaving aside certain concepts, and devote myself to furthering contributions to their serious and essential line of thought. For the same reason I shall attempt to give my own tentative reply to two aspects of the same essential question repeatedly asked by the authors: (1) On what basis or by what process the analyst selects the relevant general principle or frame of reference in the process of arriving at interpretations? (2) How to arrive systematically at the empirical test which facilitates the full development of psychoanalysis as a science of 'traces'.

Quoting Cheshire (1964) who states that Freud 'committed no grosser crime than to take determinism seriously', the authors assert that 'A strict determinism is the basis of science, any science, and is not the sole priority of the "advanced sciences".' Since I agree with that declaration, I shall begin by stating which are the elements that lead me to believe that psychoanalysis should be bound within limits in the same way as the other factual disciplines are, and then go on to give a brief outline of the scientific method which individuates it from them.

Psychoanalysis puts the canonical scientific method into effect (Klimovsky, 1971). It defines both its epistemological empirical basis, i.e. the values that everday practice offers to our understanding, and its methodological empirical basis, i.e. the presupposed theories used as a basis for its daily work. Those bases lead to the postulation of three types of scientific statement: (1) basic or primary level empirical affirmations, based on clinical observation of a single individual, a group of individuals or a basic statistical sample; (2) secondary level affirmations or empirical generalizations that clinically generalize the presence of certain properties in a universal or probabilistic manner; and (3) tertiary level affirmations or generalizations that refer to theoretical values. Psychoanalysis possesses, although it has not developed them systematically, both types of tertiary level affirmation, i.e. purely theoretical or 'theoretical type' generalizations, and mixed theoretical-empirical generalizations or 'rules of correspondence' between metapyschological theory and clinical empiricism.

[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious as Infinite Sets: An Essay in Bi-Logic

By Ignacio Matte-Blanco. London: Duckworth. 1975. Pp. 472.

Henri Rey

Henri Rey

Henri Rey  

'This book is written for psycho-analysts as well as for mathematical philosophers.' This is the opening first sentence of the preface. It is probably extremely important to make clear to those who might be tempted to read this book—and I hope they will be many—that psychoanalysts suspicious of the application of mathematical logic to psychoanalysis or worried because of their lack of basic knowledge in logico-mathematics and symbolic logic need not entertain such doubts. Through and through the book is about psychoanalysis and written by a psychoanalyst with considerable clinical experience. The logico-mathematical treatment of the subject is made easy because every logico-mathematical concept used is simple and simply explained from first principles. On the other hand, the logico-mathematical expert will find psychoanalytical principles and clinical examples also as clearly set as one could possibly desire. Because of this double preoccupation of the author one could at first sight find some aspects of the book repetitive—as the author himself says—but in fact this is not really so.

491

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler  

The 29th International Psycho-Analytical Congress took place in London from 20 to 25 July 1975, under the Presidency of Prof. Serge Lebovici. Two invited papers (by André Green and Leo Rangell) were published before the Congress in this Journal, Vol. 56, Part 1. These formed the basis for a full-day discussion at a plenary session on 'Changes in Psychoanalytic Practice and Experience: Theoretical, Technical and Social Implications'. A special feature of the London Congress was the inclusion of three simultaneous 'dialogues' on 24 July. In addition, individual scientific papers were presented, and these are included in this double issue of the Journal.

As usual, the Programme Committee undertook the heavy burden of devising the programme, choosing the papers, speakers, discussants and reporters. They also assumed the responsibility for editing the Congress material.

As Editor, I should like to express my appreciation to the Programme Committee and also to the Executive Secretary of the I.P.A., Mrs Irene Auletta, for their help in co-ordinating the arrangements for the publication of the Congress Proceedings.

1

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Donald Meltzer on 'The Delusion of Clarity of Insight'—Two Modes of Thinking and Perceiving

John Padel

John Padel

John H. Padel
12 Broadlands Road
Highgate, London N.6.

John Padel  

1. There are features of Dr Meltzer's paper (this issue) that I welcome and I will begin with these. One is his emphasis on the use of dream-material as providing the analyst with a richness of interpretative possibilities (¶5), because of the way the dream unites the internal and external worlds. A second is the notion of two modes of perception and two modes of thinking corresponding with two organizations of the mind (he calls them narcissistic and introjective but I prefer two more neutral terms like 'binary' and 'ternary' because they describe better the two-person and three-person modes of relating, in which the two organizations of the mind are based). A third is his doubt whether the shift from one mode to the other is always mediated by the mental manoeuvre that has been labelled 'projective identification'. A fourth is his conviction that different identification-processes are at the root of the two organizations of the mind.

2. I find myself strongly in agreement with his patient, Mrs D., in her belief (¶20) that evidence and the laws of evidence are more important criteria of truth-function than are aesthetic experiences. In fact, if Dr Meltzer himself did not ultimately agree with us, he would never have complained that she preferred the Oxford English Dictionary to any evidence that he could offer from his own past experience. What I miss in her case-history are certain facts that we would need in order to estimate the analytic process and its reality for his patient. The most basic of all is the time-relationship between the onset of her symptoms and the birth of her children. We do not even know whether she had had one or more children before the year in Canada or before the return to England, nor, if any were already born, what domestic help enabled Mrs D. to continue her job.

3. Failing knowledge of these and of some other facts and failing knowledge of nearly all associations to the dreams, we cannot gain a conviction that the analyses are grounded in the actual lives of Dr Meltzer's patients. Nevertheless I think that he deliberately restricts us in this way so as to make us attend to his style of interpretation and its effects and to emphasize his use of certain concepts, especially that of 'projective identification' in the phantasy of 'intruding inside the sensory apparatus and mental equipment of [one's] internal objects' (¶6). We shall have to consider if that is a useful way of describing certain mental events and attitudes.

[[RunningHead]]

Ego Functions in Schizophrenics, Neurotics and Normals: A Systematic Study of Conceptual, Diagnostic and Therapeutic Aspects

By Leopold Bellak, Marvin Hurvich and Helen K. Gediman. New York: John Wiley and Sons. 1973. Pp. 571.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg  

The objectives of the research reported in this book were to evaluate whether the systematic study of disturbances or regression in ego functions would differentiate patients diagnosed as schizophrenics by ordinary clinical criteria from neurotic and normal subjects, and whether it would be possible to group schizophrenic patients on the basis of similarities of ego-function patterns. The authors hoped that such a study would have important diagnostic and prognostic implications for the effects of various treatments for schizophrenic and other patients.

The book opens with a summary of the salient features of the psychoanalytic theory of the ego as structure and as constellation of functions, exploring the relationships among ego, id and superego, and between drives and psychic structures. The author's explicit aim is to focus on ego functions while yet keeping the ego concept within the context of psycho-analytic metapsychology. They review the evolving connexions between Freud's thinking at various stages and the ideas current today. Their focus is on the exploration of commonly agreed-upon ego functions that satisfy potential research criteria of generally agreed-upon definitions, relative closeness to behavioural observation or to immediate subjective descriptions of patients, and centrality in terms of the importance of the selected ego functions.

This approach has the advantage of linking the theoretical overview with the research design and findings that follow; in the process the authors achieve an objective view of unresolved or even contradictory aspects of psychoanalytic thinking. It has the disadvantage of sometimes overcondensing crucial contributions or concepts, and failing to resolve contradictions in these concepts (which would, admittedly, require a very detailed metapsychological analysis), resulting in possible frustrations for the reader hoping to find an integrative presentation of contemporary psychoanalytic metapsychology. Nevertheless, the very open-endedness of the presentation should be a stimulating challenge to the sophisticated psychoanalytic reader. Bellak and his co-workers also focus sharply—and appropriately, I think—on current confusions in psychoanalytic writing regarding the concept of structure and function, thus contributing significantly to the re-examination of psycho-analytic metapsychology.

4330 Sherbrooke West

James Naiman  

Victor Calef introduced the panellists and invited them to present their views.

Calef referred to a letter he had sent early in 1974 to former supervisees, former candidate analysands and former psychiatric analysands. The analysands wrote of the disappearance of their symptoms and about the differences in their relationships both to people and to their work—and the supervisees of their learning experiences, with much less reluctance about attempting to express what the learning process was. Calef contrasted the atmosphere of frustration and renunciation of the analytic situation with the atmosphere of gratification of the supervisory situation.

In Calef's view, the image of the therapist after analysis is related to post-oedipal developments. The atmosphere of frustration and renunciation of the analytic situation creates an altered state of consciousness resembling a dream state, though not identical with it. The frustrations and the renunciations of the personal analysis prepare the stage for a particular kind of learning which can be experienced in supervision. That type of learning may be conceptualized as sublimatory.

Calef then reviewed some of the salient points in the history of psychoanalytic therapy.

When it became clear that abreaction, catharsis and the return of memories were inadequate explanations for the therapeutic process, certain formulae (i.e. 'to make the unconscious conscious' and 'where id was, ego shall be') reflected the evolution of psycho-analytic theory. Analysts became more intent on describing the fate of the ego and the superego in the therapeutic process. They sought to detail the changes wrought in these structures by therapy. The Marienbad Congress of 1936 explored many of these developments in terms of change in the ego and superego. During these phases of analytic theory-building the prevalent view was that the pathology of the ego was the indirect result of sexual conflicts and the regressions instituted by them. The thought was that the therapy could expose the nature of the conflict, undo the fixation and reverse the regression to permit the ego that which it had been restricted from doing by the sexualizations. However, in the broadened clinical experience many observers believed that they were seeing another set of clinical facts, namely that their patients suffered from insults to the ego which were not necessarily the product of sexual conflicts but injuries from other sources and which were conceived as more or less primary. The controversy about these injuries to the ego questioned whether they had oedipal or pre-oedipal sources. The oldest of those developments concerned itself with the pathological potential of introjects, identifications and object relationships. More recently, clinical and theoretical interests have turned towards the problems of self- and object-representations, towards developmental problems and towards the problems of narcissism, especially in the so-called borderline pathology.

A Discussion of the Paper by H. M. Southwood on 'Analysis as Experience'

B. Easser

B. Easser

B. Ruth Easser
70 Pleasant Boulevard
Toronto 7, Canada TH 2

B. Easser  

Psychoanalysis, by necessity, has had as its baseline, in fact as its principal armamentorium, words. To permit other than highly symbolic communication within the psychoanalytic situation is to defeat the purpose and often to arouse a degree of anxiety in the patient that precludes further work. Anna Freud (1946) in describing the analytic situation and the purpose of free association stated,

The ego is … requested to be silent and the id is invited to speak and promised that its derivatives shall not encounter the usual difficulties if they emerge into consciousness. … The warrant is valid only for their translation into ideas of words: it does not entitle them to take control of the motor apparatus, … we have to play a double game with the patient's instinctual impulses. … (pp. 12–13).

She concludes that this arrangement may evoke many of the technical difficulties which we encounter.

Dr Southwood (this issue) in his analytic model would suggest that he hopes to bypass some of these difficulties. Dr Southwood, in presenting a model for the psychoanalytic process, states: 'what goes on in psychoanalysis can be more adequately as well as more simply understood by taking a two-person experiential model than by using the structural theory'. He concludes that, 'once I used to regard the transference as a sort of guiding star that I had to keep in sight, whereas now I tend to follow the shared experiencing 'whole person' relating, as it is constantly disturbed by the transferences and identifications'. Dr Southwood, by this description, would appear to be expressing the dissatisfaction of many investigators and therapists who have chosen to focus more closely on the transactions that occur between patient and therapist, the nature of this relationship, the motivations behind the therapist's interventions and confrontations. He appears to have joined the movement which is drawing further away from interpretations which rely on interactions of ego, superego and id.

Psychoanalytic Child Development Research and the Practice of General Psychiatry

Edgar Lipton

Edgar Lipton

Edgar L. Lipton
156 East 79th Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Edgar Lipton  

SUMMARY

Understanding derived from psychoanalytic child development research can lead to the application of preventative mental hygiene measures and may form the basis for modifications in techniques of psychotherapy. In addition, it is useful for theory building that may, in turn, suggest new areas of investigation.

Three interests are stressed in a discussion of the findings of psychoanalysts: (1) the effects of early physical malformations on personality development; (2) current relevance of maternal deprivation studies; and (3) the usefulness of Margaret Mahler's concepts of child development for the understanding and treatment of more disturbed patients.

Anne Wylie

140a Foster Street

Anne Stiles Wylie
CAMBRIDGE, MASS

Anne Wylie  

With varying emphasis biographers of Vincent van Gogh have pointed out the solitariness and isolation which thread as a central motif through his life. Van Gogh's infancy and childhood must have been affected by being a replacement for a stillborn first child, also named Vincent, who had been born exactly one year earlier. Little is known of van Gogh's early childhood except for anecdotal evidence that he was often troublesome and self-willed, that he was indulged by 'tender-hearted parents', and that, independently, a number of schoolmates years later recalled his solitary ways. As van Gogh grew out of adolescence, he strove desperately to find a person or cause or calling which would give him peace and fulfilment. Throughout his life he struggled with melancholia and repeatedly identified himself with the miserable and forlorn.

When asked as a young man if a beautiful woman or girl would attract him, van Gogh had answered: 'I would feel more attraction for and would rather come in contact with one who was ugly or old, or in some way unhappy, but who through experience and sorrow had gained a mind and a soul (117, I, p. 160)1. The appeal he always felt for those who had suffered or were in trouble helps to account for van Gogh's unreasonable and extravagant love for his older widowed cousin, Kee. He was 28 years old then, living with his parents and finishing a year's self-directed study of perspective and drawing. Kee, stern faced and far from pretty, was a caring mother to her eight-year-old first-born son when Vincent fell in love with her. A second son had died as an infant four years before. Although faced with an immediate and adamant 'no, no never, ' van Gogh was obsessed in pursuing his courtship.

Despite the hopelessness of his suit, van Gogh worked energetically, in a state of near-euphoria suggestive of a manic denial of rejection and loss.

… it was at first as terrible a blow as a death sentence, and for a moment it absolutely crushed me to the ground.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of the Paper by David Liberman on 'Changes in the Theory and Practice of Psychoanalysis'

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth T. Calder
110 East 78th Street
New York. N.Y. 10021

Kenneth Calder  

Although it is tempting to discuss Dr Liberman's paper (this issue) point-by-point as he presents his ideas, I believe it will be more useful if I follow an organization somewhat different from his but with a clear intent to discuss most if not all of the issues he describes. It is my aim to focus on what I consider Dr Liberman's most important topic: what is essential in psychoanalysis that we wish to preserve in spite of changes in society or patient population and/or advances in psychoanalytic theory and technique.

I share with Wallerstein (1969) the view that psychoanalysis is the method 'which analyses transference and resistance back to their genetic-dynamic roots in the infantile psychic life'. The primary aim of the psychoanalytic process is for the analysand to learn how his mind functions and how it came to function that way. To achieve this aim, it is essential that the patient accomplish several analytic objectives (or perhaps 'assignments' or 'tasks'). My discussion of Liberman's paper will be organized around four essential objectives: motivation, free association, insight and change.

First, the patient has to be motivated to learn about himself or herself. Ideally the patient suffers from his emotional problems and, because of pain, is willing to explore his inner life to the fullest in the strong anticipation that his suffering will be relieved through understanding. Liberman reports that, unfortunately, 'the bulk of patients' now come with a weak motivation; namely, they are 'quasi-colleagues' who want to be analysed, at least in large measure, for narcissistic gain or as an education by means of which they can function better in their profession. To change their motivation, such as by demonstrating ways in which they do indeed suffer from their emotional problems, becomes a primary objective of the analysis.

That this is not a hopeless task is implied by two recent studies on training analyses, comparable with but not identical to 'quasicolleague' analyses. One study, by D. Shapiro (1974) of the Columbia University Psychoanalytic Clinic, was based on a questionnaire and found that 104 analysts out of 122 who responded 'viewed their own therapeutic gains as satisfactory and one third of the entire sample … gave a highly favorable view of their analytic gains'. The second study, under the chairmanship of M. Stein (1974) and conducted by a Study Group of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education of the American Psychoanalytic Association, was done by way of interviews with experienced training analysts and with recent graduates.

[[RunningHead]]

The Overt Homosexual

By Charles W. Socarides. New York and London: Grune & Stratton. 1968. Pp. vii + 245.

Stanley Steinberg

Stanley Steinberg

Stanley Steinberg  

In The Overt Homosexual Dr Socarides brings together his impressive work of two decades on the genesis, psychodynamics and psychoanalytic treatment of homosexuality. A great asset of the book is its comprehensive survey of psychoanalytic knowledge concerning homosexuality prior to recent developmental theory. Dr Socarides notes that the focus of much of Freud's research into both male and female homosexuality was on vicissitudes of the Oedipus complex and the ubiquity of castration fears. Throughout his research Freud discussed homosexuality in terms of libidinal and ego development. He also considered whether constitutional factors played an important part in alterations of normal sexual functions, but he did not exclude individual psychological issues in the development of a homosexual fixation. For example, he pointed to the importance of individual experience upon later sexual development such as early seduction or castration threats made upon vulnerable individuals in phase-specific times and the influence of parental constellations upon sexual indentifications. He also brought attention to social factors influencing sexual preferences; for instance, sexual development in various cultures and historical periods and sexual development in current times in unusual setting, such as in war and during incarcerations.

The essential developmental factor in male homosexuality was a strong fixation upon the mother leading to identification with her and a search for themselves as a narcissistic sexual object. Freud also pointed out that the strong fixation led to an incestuous overattachment, castration fears and, as protection, the longed-for narcissistic object choice. He indicated in his formulations the importance such identifications and ego mechanisms had upon later ego development.

Socarides integrates current developmental theory and ego psychology into his conception of homosexuality. The central thesis of the book is the pregenital origin of all overt homosexuality and the genesis of homosexuality in disturbances of the earliest object relationships.

Discussion of 'The Role of Family Life in Child Development'—On 'Father Formation' in Early Child Development

Eugenio Gaddini

Eugenio Gaddini

Eugenio Gaddini
Via Sebastiano Conca 13
Rome 00197

Eugenio Gaddini  

After reading the papers by Maurice Friend (this issue) and Horst-Eberhard Richter (this issue), I found myself thinking of the word 'familiar' and of its being associated with the idea of family as something one is supposed to be well acquainted with. This is certainly not so for the growing child, for whom the task of becoming familiar with his family's structure and life is certainly immense. The unsolved mysteries and riddles of early life may later turn into psychopathology, and sometimes into creative research and expression. Once indeed they gave rise to no less than psychoanalysis itself.

Since the suggestion of the Programme Committee has been to discuss the topic of this Dialogue, rather than the excellent papers which have been given by Friend and Richter, I shall take this opportunity to make an attempt to approach what is for the child (and partly still for us, too) one of the basic mysteries, namely the father. I refer to the psychological role of the father in early child development.

While, on the sociological side, the role of the father has undergone striking changes in this century—at least in our culture—his psychological role in my view, does not seem to have changed, and possibly is not going to change in the foreseeable future. I am obviously not referring to the many ways in which his role may be distorted, rather than changed, within different family situations, but to what the father basically means to the growing child and, before then, to the child's mother.

In fact, from the very moment a woman knows that she is expecting a baby, her relation to herself and to her husband changes. She becomes more self-centred, less concerned with her husband as her lover and shows a greater need of tender affection. She wants to share her 'primary maternal preoccupation', as Winnicott (1957) has described it, with him, and she needs to be relieved and protected from external preoccupations. Her relation to the baby growing inside, the way she experiences the gradual changes in her body, her expectations concerning the actual delivery and the baby's appearance, are certainly influenced by her husband's attitude towards her. All this is just to say that the father's role, as well as the mother's, begins to exist before the child is born, although the father cannot help being left out from the mother–child bodily coexistence. His influence on the child to be born lies in his capacity to influence the mother's attitude towards the baby inside her body.

[[RunningHead]]

On Wanting Too Much: The 'Exceptions' Revisited

Anton Kris

Anton Kris

Anton O. Kris
37 Philbrick Road
Brookline, Mass. 02146

Anton Kris  

SUMMARY

Freud's description of the 'exceptions' is reviewed in the light of accumulated clinical experience with 'narcissistic' patients and from the perspective of the distinction between active and passive libidinal aims. Analysis of narcissistic patients often reaches a point at which they conclude that they 'want too much'. This insatiability is attributed in the discussion to a block to satisfaction of passive libidinal wishes and a consequent overemphasis upon active libidinal wishes. An analogy is drawn to the metabolic block of diabetes mellitus, which produces starvation. This formulation is applied to the 'exceptions' to account for a number of clinical observations. The main anxieties and guilt that lead to the block to satisfaction of passive wishes are considered. The central role of unconscious guilt is emphasized, and some of the problems encountered in the attempt to make the patient aware of it are elaborated. The mechanism by which unconscious guilt over active libidinal and aggressive wishes leads to defences against passive libidinal wishes, owing to failure of signal guilt and inadequacy of ego ideal functioning, is described. Implications for treatment are outlined, and one aspect of the psychoanalytic process is related to the block to passive wishes: the gratifying experience of being understood.
