responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice—Dreams and Free Association

Harold Blum

Harold Blum

Harold P. Blum
23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, New York 11576

Harold Blum  

Dreams have been the subject of extensive investigation through psychoanalysis since its inception and more recently by experimental studies of sleep and the psychophysiology of the dream/sleep state (E. Hartmann, 1967). Questions have arisen concerning the biological and even the teleological role of the dream. For example, do we dream in order to sleep, do we sleep in order to dream, is the dream a normal concomitant and possible preserver only of REM sleep, or are we not yet in a position to state decisively what the relationship is between psychological dream, phases of sleep, and other altered states of consciousness? The upsurge of dream and sleep studies have spurred efforts at interdisciplinary integration and re-evaluation of psychoanalytic dream theory. The remarkable and important laboratory research on dreaming sleep has nevertheless added very little to the understanding of dreaming as a psychological phenomenon or to the clinical significance of the dream in psycho-analysis. Within the framework of psycho-analytic psychology, Freud's masterful conceptions and insights into the dream have been so rich and relatively complete that new additions to dream theory have been very limited. During the rapid growth of our entire science, the dream has remained a most solid and fundamental keystone.

In recent years there has been little evolutionary change in the clinical use of dreams, and the title of this dialogue might be misleading. But it is illuminating to consider whether dreams are used in the same way, with the same frequency and purpose as in the past, before the ascendance of modern ego psychology. In the shift from the topographic to structural theory, it was recognized that like all psychic products, the dream is subject to the influence of all three agencies. As ego psychology developed, a more subtle understanding of the significance of the dream in the analytic process was possible, as well as parallel attention to other varied and vitally important clinical phenomena (Rangell, 1956). In this paper, I shall particularly emphasize the dream within free association as a continuing framework for the analytic study of dreaming and of the dream.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Female Sexuality—The Consideration of Some Blind Spots in the Exploration of the 'Dark Continent'

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel
82 Rue de l'Université
75007 Paris, France.

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel  

Because time is limited it will not be possible for me to deal with Freud's ideas on female sexuality in their entirety, even less to compare them with opposing views expressed by other psychoanalysts. I shall therefore speak about only those issues which have caused the greatest controversy.

Let me open this discussion with a remark of a general nature: if a subject as fundamental as female sexuality causes such disagreement among analysts after almost 80 years of clinical experience, it must be because it stirs up certain internal factors in a particularly intense way which somehow interfere with our progress towards knowledge. Our differences of opinion about female sexuality are such that in the mêlée we lose sight of the truth.

A correlative comment comes to mind: divergencies in our understanding of female sexuality inevitably breed corresponding differences of opinion concerning male sexuality. Bisexuality, the notion of a 'complete' Oedipus—both negative and positive—the necessity for dual identification, all conspire to cast the shadow of the 'dark Continent' on to male sexuality. It seems to me artificial and fallacious to completely abstract the study of female sexuality from that of the femininity common to both sexes and of human sexuality in general.

I shall therefore restrict my study of Freud's work on female sexuality to the discussion of some essential points; but at the same time I find myself forced to broaden the scope of these very same issues.

I shall examine the theory of sexual phallic monism and its most important consequences; I shall try to formulate certain hypotheses which I have developed on other occasions, with the idea in mind of showing how Freudian theories of female sexuality have endured in spite of the opposing clinical material which has come to light, in spite of the undeniable contradictions these theories reveal, and finally in spite of those theories which have lent a completely different dimension to female sexuality. I shall express a personal view on these matters, particularly on the topic of penis envy; yet my study will remain very much within a Freudian perspective; my basic assumptions being rooted in the question of 'human prematurity', which is linked to the child's early helplessness (Hilflosigkeit).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel on 'The Changing Expectations of Patients and Psychoanalysts Today'

Bertrand Cramer and Olivier Flournoy

Olivier Flournoy

Bertrand Cramer
Olivier Flournoy
Av. de Champel 45
1206 Geneva

Olivier Flournoy  

The Chairman, Wolfgang Loch, suggested some topics which might be discussed in connexion with the title of this panel:

1. Do we still have the same expectations now as we had previously in terms of the goals of psychoanalysis, for example to translate the id into the ego?

2. Are we still satisfied with such therapeutic expectations as a gain in the capacity for love and work?

3. Do we think that unconscious expectations regarding gains through psychoanalysis have changed, or do we believe that conscious expectations only have changed?

4. Have our psychoanalytic models changed deeply, or are the changes in our theoretical and technical models only minimal?

5. Can we say that there have been changes in the psychoanalytic setting, or are Freud's basic paradigms essentially unchanged?

To these basic questions various members of the panel attempted to give some answers; the contributions from the floor, however, seemed to focus more on the problem of change rather than on changing expectations, and many speakers addressed themselves mainly to social change.

There were three clearly discernible tendencies in the discussions: (1) contributions addressed mostly to changes in social circumstances with their influences on the patient, on the analyst and on the type of demand for psychoanalytic treatment; (2) those which dealt with the problem of changes in the types of psychopathology encountered in analytic practice today; and (3) addresses which dealt with the problem of changing expectations from the intrapsychic point of view as well as changes in our theoretical framework which might influence these expectations.

If the problems were more often dealt with from the point of view of changes and not changing expectations, it was possibly because the large audience characteristic of international meetings does not easily allow the uncovering of subjective, personal expectations.

1. Changes in social circumstances

Several contributors spoke about the changing world, its influences on analysis, and particularly on the mode of request for analysis. In these discussions, the speakers treated the psycho-analytic situation exclusively from without, centring their attention on political, sociological and economical aspects, namely on the problem of power relationships, from the level of national politics down to the individual family nucleus.

[[RunningHead]]

The Mutual Influences of the Positive Cohesive Self, Mental Representational Structures and Interactive Behaviour in the Child's Involvement with Peers

Thomas Horner , Mary Whiteside and Fred Busch

Fred Busch

Thomas M. Horner
Children's Psychiatric Hospital, University of Michigan Medical Center
Ann Arbor, Mich. 48104
Mary F. Whiteside
Fred Busch
ANN ARBOR, MICH.

Fred Busch  

SUMMARY

In this presentation we have offered some ways of conceptualizing the role played by cognitive factors in the development of the toddler and preschool child's interactive skills. Starting with the concept of mental-representational structure we have outlined cognitive processes whose operation we have inferred from observation of interactions between age-mates in a nursery setting. In observing three sets of such processes (planning, identification of social cues and cognitive matching) we have also attempted to acknowledge the importance of a developing core of positive feelings about the self within the child. The formation of the positive cohesive self structure has been viewed not only as an integral component of this development but also as a beneficiary of the interactive skills which emerge from the young child's interactions with age-mates. The mutual contributions made by the emergence and continued growth of these skills and narcissistic equilibrium was discussed as a key characteristic of the developmental line from ego-centricity to companionship.

Young Children in Brief Separation—A Critical Review of Five Films by James Robertson and Joyce Robertson

Hansi Kennedy

Hansi Kennedy

Hansi Kennedy
13 Compayne Gardens.
London NW6 3DG

Hansi Kennedy  

The last in a series of documentary films on young children's reaction to separation from home while the mother is in hospital for the birth of a second child, has just been released. The film, Lucy, Twenty-One Months, in Foster Care for Nineteen Days, attempts to show us, condensed into 30 minutes, how a young child lives through such a stressful situation, expertly cared for and supported by her fostermother Joyce Robertson, and expertly filmed by the cameraman, James Robertson.

The commentary tells us that Lucy's early development was normal and that she had received adequate mothering. At age 21 months her body control was excellent, she played intelligently and constructively but she did not yet talk or even say 'mummy'. During preparatory interchange visits prior to the separation we get a glimpse of Lucy with her mother both reflecting a rather sombre mood in their almost identical facial expressions; from the commentary we learn that this might be related to mother's slightly depressed mood during the latter stages of the pregnancy. We see the same expression on Lucy's face for the first couple of days following her arrival in the Robertson home; but, contrary to what one might expect, she subsequently becomes increasingly lively and cheerful over the first week in fostercare. There is an increasingly warm interchange between her and the fostermother and Lucy's behaviour and expression reveal a whole range of feelings towards the fostermother, from petulance and anger to gaiety and affection; moreover this change in affective expression is also apparent in relation to her father on his daily visits.

Probably everyone watching this film will agree that Lucy seems happier by the end of the first week in fostercare than immediately before the separation. Not everyone, however, will interpret this in the same way since in itself this apparent change does not tell us much about her feelings towards her mother. Again, we get no conclusive indication about the nature of her feelings towards mother from Lucy's reaction to the photograph of herself and her mother which she has brought along with her personal possessions; although on one occasion she hides her eyes when shown the photograph, at other times it does not elicit any meaningful response.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the Work of Bion

By Leon Grinberg, Dario Sor, Elizabeth Tabak de Bianchedi. Translated from the Spanish by Alberto Hahn. Scotland: Clunie Press, publishers to the Roland Harris Educational Trust. 1975. Pp. 82.

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle  

There is a fairly wide-spread belief among analysts, which I share, that Bion's works are both very important and very difficult to understand. Moreover, the difficulty is felt to reside in the novelty of the notions introduced rather than in any obscurity of expression, which is expected to become clear as soon as the novel notions are recognized for what they are.

The book to be reviewed is a summary of Bion's works arranged in such a way that an understanding of one notion is likely to help the reader to understand the next one to be introduced. And I think this aim has been very well achieved and that, by this linking of one new concept with another and by the general discussion of them, Bion's works are made a good deal easier to understand. And if the reader, as I did, still finds some notions difficult, perhaps this is a salutary experience. For it makes one realize that however much analysis one thinks one knows, there is always an infinite amount which remains unknown to one—in other words, that psychoanalysis is an endless quest.

Whether this review of a review, or 'transformation of a transformation' to use one of Bion's terms, will make it any easier, I do not know. But as a start it occurs to me that some of his notions become easier if one remembers that much of what he is writing about is that mysterious thing called instinct, particularly the cognative aspect of it which is now, for the first time, being psychoanalytically explored. For instance, although he expressly calls them instinctive, it is easy to forget that the 'basic assumptions' which can, he believes, be observed in a group, are in his view instinctive. In fact, I think they are the same sort of things that he calls 'innate preconceptions' in individual psychology. In other words, I think that just as there is an innate preconception of a breast which mates with the realization of being fed by a real breast or bottle to form a conception of one, so in a group there are basic assumptions that the 'dependent group' is there to be nurtured by someone, the 'fight-flight group' to fight or flee and the 'pairing group' eugenically to create the next generation—that is, to breed a hero. Presumably, something in the actual setting determines which 'basic assumption' will be recognized as active.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of 'The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice'—The Dream as a 'Royal Road' to the Biology of the Mental Apparatus

Ernest Hartmann

Ernest Hartmann

Ernest Hartmann
Director, Sleep and Dream Laboratory, Boston State Hospital
Boston, Mass. 02124

Ernest Hartmann  

I shall here consider the dream as a 'royal road' (via regia) but I shall attempt to follow the road somewhat further than has been customary. I believe the dream may serve as a royal road to the unconscious wishes and conflicts of the dreamer, certainly; and it can lead to a broader general knowledge of the human unconscious; in addition I shall try to show that the dream can lead to a psychological and biological understanding of waking psychic structures, and thence to a knowledge of the biology of the mental apparatus. I shall only be able here to map roughly the direction and destination of this road.

I shall follow an approach here based to some extent on the work of Hughlings Jackson, the great neurologist whose name is not frequently seen in a psychoanalytic journal. Jackson saw the brain as a series of concentric layers from the lowest functions 'inside' to the highest functions at the top or 'outside'; the principle he enunciated to which I want to call attention is that any neurological illness involving damage to so-called higher centres produces two kinds of symptoms: it reveals or unmasks something of the underlying 'lower' structure and it also tells us something about what has been removed. Thus for a neurological condition which involves injury to some 'higher' function, we can learn something about the 'lower' centres which have been unmasked, and we can also learn something about the 'higher' functions which have been injured or removed.

It may be worth looking at the dream in this way. The dream of course is not an illness or an injury, in fact we have have considerable evidence that dreams, or at least dreaming sleep, may be useful in waking life; I have presented elsewhere a theory of the functions of dreaming sleep (Hartmann, 1973). None the less, the dream is a state of the mental apparatus which obviously reveals a great deal of interest and, equally obviously, the dream is a state differing from waking in that something has been removed.

[[RunningHead]]

Dialogue on 'The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice'

Homer Curtis and David Sachs

David Sachs

Homer C. Curtis
111 North Forty-Ninth Street
Philadelphia, Pa. 19139
David M. Sachs
PHILADELPHIA

David Sachs  

This Dialogue on dreams continued the development of the theme of the Congress which was to demonstrate significant differences among analysts in their theoretical and clinical concepts. In the most extreme instances these differences brought into question the very definition and purpose of psychoanalysis. The following report will indicate how widely divergent are the conceptual orientations of the speakers in regard to dreams, a topic fundamental to psycho-analysis. It was hoped that the selection of advocates of differing positions would result in a dialogue; however, it was seldom possible to maintain the interchange of ideas necessary to establish that process. For this reason, the reader will not find many examples of a developing interaction among the speakers. Instead, this report will demonstrate that speakers and discussants tended to use their time to develop the arguments which supported their own positions. They accomplished this with sufficient clarity so that those in attendance were able to become aware of the differences of opinion and could use this opportunity to clarify their own thinking. It is the hope of the reporters that they have reflected the proceedings of the meeting in such a way that the reader may have the same experience.

In his opening remarks the Chairman, Jean-Bertrand Pontalis (Paris) reminded the audience that the theme of the Dialogue was not dreams per se, but the changing use of dreams in clinical practice. He pointed out that among the major theories of psychoanalysis, the theory of dreams has changed the least, although the dream is variously seen as a message, a puzzle to be deciphered or an intrapsychic experience. He then introduced Harold Blum (New York) who delivered the first presentation (Blum, this issue) of the morning.

Blum began by observing that the intensive laboratory research on dreaming sleep has added little to the understanding of dreaming as a psychological phenomenon. Within analysis itself, Freud's insights were so rich that new additions to dream theory have been quite limited. In a discussion of changes in the clinical use of dreams, Blum recalled that the first analysts sought rapid revelation of unconscious content through the dream, the same goal originally sought with hypnosis. Dreams came to be used more judiciously than in those early years when 'the technique of analysis was almost synonymous with the technique of dream interpretation', as Ella Freeman Sharpe observed in 1936. Blum stated that the addition of the structural point of view led to a reassessment of the role of the dream in clinical practice. It came to be understood that there is no royal road to the unconscious without resistance. Blum observed that increasingly, analysis of neurosis followed the development of the transference neurosis, and quoted Freud's statement that 'finally every conflict has to be fought out in the sphere of the transference.' The transference meaning of all the patient's productions, including dreams, became the central focus in the recapitulation of pathogenic conflicts.

[[RunningHead]]

The Transsexual Experiment. Vol. II. of Sex and Gender

By Robert Stoller. London: The Hogarth Press and The Institute of Psycho-Analysis. 1975. (The International Psycho-Analytical Library no. 101.) Pp. 316.

Juliet Mitchell

Juliet Mitchell

Juliet Mitchell  

Although Vol. I. of Sex and Gender had a wider range of reference than the present volume, it was already clear that transsexualism was the centrepoint of Robert Stoller's argument. This volume is devoted to the transsexual experiment.

Briefly, there are two strands to Stoller's thesis. First, he argues that transsexualism with its repudiation of biological sex is a perfect point of departure from which to understand the formation of psychological masculinity and femininity (Stoller calls the psychological formation 'gender development' and restricts 'sex' to the biological). Stoller's second concern is to account for the psychodynamics of transsexualism itself.

On the larger issue of masculinity and femininity, Stoller bases his argument on a redefinition of bisexuality. He sees Freud's use of the concept as being tied inextricably (and mistakenly) to a 'biological bedrock'. Because the atrophied gonads of the alternative sex are contained within everyone there is hypothetically the organic possibility of being the other sex. For such a biological bedrock, Stoller would substitute a primary condition produced by what he somewhat reluctantly calls 'imprinting'. This in Stoller's interpretation, is a process whereby the external world is emplaced within the infant. The transsexual whose psychological gender and biological sex are at odds, illustrates Stoller's thesis that in the field of masculinity and femininity it is not biology but conditioning that is determinate. Stoller contends that there is a psychodynamic and aetiological difference between male and female transsexualism, they are not two sides of the same coin. It is the process involved in male transsexualism that has the wider implication for normal gender development.

A rough reconstruction of the aetiology of male transsexualism that Stoller hypothesizes runs like this: (I have, for reasons of economy of presentation, focused on the transsexual's mother where Stoller's main perspective is from the point of view of the transsexual). There is a depressed and withdrawn maternal grandmother who failed to 'mother' her daughter; a grandfather who for a brief but crucial period gave this daughter a love that encouraged her identification with him. This daughter (the transsexual's mother) is left with a strong bisexual disposition. In adulthood she forms an utterly empty heterosexual relationship with her husband (patient's father) who is very absent from the family and wants her son both to be her feminized phallus (her own father identification) and to finally provide her with a satisfying mother. The main point is that the mother and her son find their 'oneness' completely gratifying and the son becomes feminine like his mother.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of 'Freud and Female Sexuality'

William Grossman

William Grossman

William I. Grossman
Montefiore Hospital and Medical Center
111 East 210 Street
Bronx, New York 10467

William Grossman  

The lively current re-examination of female sexuality suggests many new perspectives and starting points. In considering how I might add something to the comprehensive contributions of Chasseguet-Smirgel (this issue) and Moore (this issue), a number of possibilities occurred to me in the form of titles or headlines. For instance, 'Sexuality is our Mythology', on the way children and scientists create myths about sex and why this is inevitable since myth, fantasy and science are ways of constructing meaningful reality. Or, another title: 'Are there Psychical Consequences to the Anatomical Distinction Between the Sexes?' Its corollary would be 'Why Anatomy is Usually Destiny' since psychosexual development means giving some meaning to the observed anatomical differences. This would suggest still another title, something like 'What Kind of Destiny Is Anatomy?' since in the long run this is a question that both children and scientists must answer. Of course, all of these themes deal with the nature and basis of sexual knowledge, which might be called an epistemological problem, but one that is double-edged. That is, they deal with the exquisitely psychoanalytic problem of how the child's problem of discovering and understanding the anatomical distinction between the sexes becomes the analyst's problem of understanding the psychical situation involved in it. A child tries to make sense out of a world that he or she experiences. These efforts, fantasy creations and play, are the child's theories, in the same sense as Freud compared Schreber's delusions to his own libido theory. In this sense, the instincts are our mythology. They are a part of our psychoanalytic effort to understand the child's misunderstanding and the reasons for it's persistence into adulthood.

To put the problem in this way is the same as saying that, first, the child must discover a biological (in this case anatomical) fact; then, that this fact is a characteristic of being a girl or a boy; then, what it means to be a girl or a boy. In the long run, this usually includes for the child a great many ideas about how the fact of being a girl or a boy seems to mean 'automatically', that you are or should be a certain way, think a certain way, and should be treated a certain way. I hope that it is evident, in this area of many misunderstandings, that I am not suggesting that there is some innate, inherent or inevitable meaning attached to being a boy or a girl, however much this may seem to be so to the child or, indeed, to most adults. In describing this process as an epistemological problem, I am suggesting instead that in every place and at every time there is some set of expectations attached to being a male or a female. What I am attempting to describe is not how some particular set of meanings is acquired, but how any set of characteristics associated with masculinity or femininity comes to seem inevitable to those who acquire them.

[[RunningHead]]

Plenary Session on 'Changes in Psychoanalytic Practice and Experience: Theoretical, Technical and Social Implications'

Leonard Shengold and James McLaughlin

James McLaughlin

Leonard Shengold
245 E. 87th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028
James T. McLaughlin
PITTSBURGH

James McLaughlin  

The day's proceedings began with presentations by the authors of the prepublished papers (Green, 1975); (Rangell, 1975).

André Green stated his intention of discussing Rangell's paper interspersed with a review of his own contribution on the subject of change in psychoanalysis. He saw Freud's work as an example of the optimal analytic stance towards continual change in our field, in that it embodied two points of view: the 'classical' and the 'openness to new ideas'. The classical analysis remains an essential tool; but difficulties with our patients have forced us to think differently and to search for new ideas and new techniques. Our disputes are over the interpretation of the facts the patients present. Rangell's frame of reference from within which he has made his contributions reflects the genetic and adaptive points of view, the structural hypothesis. Green saw his own frame of reference as different, involving especially psychoanalytic concepts also based upon Freud's work but developed by analysts working in France and England. Should we simply add to our analytic models, or should we try to find new ones? New models are preferable for the new kinds of patients we have been seeing—patients who have presented us with different mental functioning, with different relationships, with different psychological 'space'. Rather than merely connecting the unknown with the known, as Rangell would favour, Green would prefer to attempt to understand the new and unknown phenomena in these patients that does not necessarily have to be connected with what is already known.

We must come to grips with much change in the external world that we are puzzled by, but also we as analysts have changed and our ways of looking at things have changed. It may be, as Rangell says, that the id is changeless—but our views on the id have changed.

Green reviewed his contribution, characterizing it as a hybrid of his own ideas and an overview of the work of others. He had tried to present the complexity of the subject, even at the expense of clarity. He had started with the changes in our patients and in ourselves as analysts. His working hypothesis is that changes have taken place primarily in the analyst—that the changes we note in our patients are perceptible to us because we have changed and are able to perceive more in our patients. We have been forced to changed perceptiveness because many of the patients we analysts now see tend to make far heavier demands. With neurotic patients, no new models are needed; but in the vast field of the loosely called 'borderline' patients who stretch the limits of analysability, Freud's models are not enough—new concepts and terms are needed. As we proceeded with the study of these borderline patients, we needed to learn what was going on in them from the vantage point of paying more attention to what was going on within the analyst treating them. This has become equal in importance to the patient's contribution to the analytic situation.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the Scientific Programme of the 29th International Psycho-Analytical Congress

Pearl King

Pearl King

Pearl King
10 Lyndale Avenue
Child's Hill
London NW2 2PY

Pearl King  

Mr President, Miss Freud (our Honorary President), and colleagues: I think this is the first time that the Chairman of the Programme Committee has tried to convey to the Congress which they have planned, some of the thinking and the rationale behind the ultimate programme with which you are presented.

First, however, it gives me great pleasure, on behalf of all members of the Programme Committee and of the American Sub-Committee of the Programme Committee, to welcome you all to the 29th Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association. This honour would not, however, have fallen to me, but for the untimely death of my colleague and friend, Dr Wally Joffe, the original Chairman of the Committee, and I feel it is appropriate at the beginning of this Congress to let you know that the topic and shape of this Congress programme owe much to his creative and dynamic leadership of the Programme Committee during the first nine months of its existence, as well as to Dr Ed Weinshel and his colleagues in the Americas. Dr Green, my Co-Chairman, and I had the easier task of monitoring what had originally been arranged.

I will now share with you some of the thinking and the rationale behind the programme which has been worked out for this Congress.

Two guiding principles have played a major role in determining the Congress topics and the manner in which they will be arranged. In the first place, the Programme Committee wanted to ensure that the 29th Congress would be centred around vital issues which were topical and which played an essential role in every psychoanalyst's professional life. Secondly, it seemed essential to devise a forum in which it would be possible to facilitate a real exchange of views, not only between invited speakers but also between those who happen to be on the platform and all other Congress participants.

To meet our first criterion, the idea emerged of selecting topics which could be approached from different points of view, each of which was held by a significant number of psychoanalysts. We decided that emphasis should be placed on a dialogue between those who subscribed to changing approaches to psychoanalytical experience and practice, and those who represented a more traditional approach to psychoanalytical experience and practice. We agreed that the unifying theme, which would enable the areas of concern to psychoanalysts to be explored in depth should be: 'Changes in Psychoanalytic Practice and Experience: Theoretical, Technical and Social Implications'.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Ricardo Avenburg and Marcos Guiter on 'The Concept of Truth in Psychoanalysis'

Waldemar Zusman

Waldemar Zusman

Waldemar Zusman
Joaquim Nabuco, 250/601
Rio de Janeiro GB

Waldemar Zusman  

As my time is limited, I can only touch on a few of the points raised in Avenburg and Guiter's discussion (this issue) of this important subject. One of the aspects that interests me especially is the way in which the conviction that a construction is true is felt by the patient.

We all know what it cost Freud to discover that the reports of infant seduction from his adult patients were not based on external reality. This raised the whole problem of truth in psychoanalysis as a direct challenge. Seduction as a trauma from real experience had to give way to new theories and a deeper understanding of psychic reality, wherein fantasy is recognized to be on a par with truth for the patient, though the fantasy is not, in fact, true in any full sense.

As the psychoanalytical process is essentially a search for truth, or for what truth there is in the analysand's account, it is most important to know how we have arrived at this truth using the therapeutic means at our disposal.

In 'The Question of a Weltanschauung' Freud (1933) takes the criterion of truth as correspondence with the outside world. According to this definition, truth is the relationship between the fact and its representation in the mind, linked by correspondence. Often, what the analysand tells us lacks this correspondence to a greater or lesser degree, since defensive distortion has been at work. The analyst's job is to use intuition to sense the nature of this distortion and offer the analysand an interpretation which will reinstate factual truth.

In 'Constructions in Analysis' Freud (1937) points out that the analyst's conjectures become convictions in the analysand's mind, although the process begun by interpretation is not always successful in restoring the repressed memories to the patient. Freud goes on to say that if the analysis is properly conducted the analysand feels a definite conviction of the truth of the construction given to him, and this achieves the same therapeutic result as a recaptured memory. How this can be and what circumstances make it possible, Freud leaves open for later investigation, not fulfilled by him.

A Discussion of the Paper by Myron Eichler on 'The Psychoanalytic Treatment of an Hysterical Character with Special Emphasis on Problems of Aggression'

Danilo Perestrello

Danilo Perestrello

Danilo Perestrello
Praia do Russell 694
60 andar
ZC-01 Gloria
20, 000 Rio de Janeiro, Gb.

Danilo Perestrello  

The opportunity to discuss Dr Eichler's paper (this issue) gives me both pleasure and satisfaction for several reasons. The first of these is the clarity with which the author has expressed his thoughts, showing a solid knowledge of psychoanalytical theory. Another satisfying point is that the paper under discussion affords us an excellent example of how analysis is practised differently in different parts of the world. The predominant orientation in South America, as is well known, is Kleinian and while I do not consider myself a Kleinian, I nevertheless feel that I have not remained totally immune to that particular current. Accepting that no science can flourish outside its own culture, it is interesting to observe how the same science—psychoanalysis in this case—develops diversely in distinct areas of the same culture. However, since no two patients drawn from any culture are alike, and since the same holds true for us analysts, it follows that my remarks will reveal a highly personal view of Dr Eichler's paper.

In order to make my comments more readily understandable, I should like to state that the dominant outlook in current psychology and psychopathology strikes me as being of the utmost importance. I am referring to the conceptualization of the human being as a whole in permanent relationship to the other; a view to which Freud made a fundamental contribution, but one to which M. Klein and Fairbairn gave undeniable depth in their emphasis on object relations. Mention must also be made of a similar vision in thinkers alien to the field of psychoanalysis, more precisely those philosophers who revived F. Brentano's notion of intentionality; I refer, of course, to Husserl's epistemological and Heidegger's ontological contributions.

The details of all these authors' vision are far less important than the view considered as a whole, in the sense of releasing ourselves from an intrapsychic psychology in order to treat man as a being who is open to the world. In this world he functions in relationship to both internal and external objects, which leads us to a psychology of object relations; self and object being considered in a permanent feedback which builds up a structure, a gestalt.

C. Hanly and J. Masson

Dept. of Philosophy University of Toronto
Toronto, Canada M5S 1A1
J. Masson
Dept. of Sanscrit and Indian Studies University of Toronto
Toronto, Canada M5S 1A1

J. Masson  

SUMMARY

The above considerations of some clinical and applied psychoanalytic material bearing on the problem of narcissism leads us to the view (1) that narcissistic libidinal organizations and object libidinal organizations are dynamically interdependent; (2) that the so-called higher transformations of narcissism (cosmic narcissism) mask libido regressed to or fixated at pre-oedipal stages of development; and (3) that narcissism, whether in its higher or other forms, has the effect of reducing

61

the quantity of libido available for object relations and life.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychological Birth of the Human Infant: Symbiosis and Individuation

By Margaret S. Mahler, Fred Pine and Anni Bergman. New York: Basic Books. 1975. Pp. 308.

Anne-Marie Sandler

Anne-Marie Sandler

Anne-Marie Sandler  

Margaret Mahler is one of the great psycho-analytic observers of our time. Students of her many previous publications are familiar with her approach to the study of childhood psychosis, an area of work which culminated in her book on infantile psychosis (On Human Symbiosis and the Vicissitudes of Individuation. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1968). Although it has been clear for many years that the detailed studies conducted and reported by Mahler and her colleagues at the Master's Children's Centre in New York contained an implicit psychoanalytic psychology of normal development, it is only now that a full volume has been devoted to this importnat subject. In The Psychological Birth of the Human Infant Margaret Mahler and two of her colleagues provide a wealth of observational data and theoretical conclusions which demonstrate unequivocally that the important periods in child development are not confined to the first year of life nor only to the oedipal period. Moreover, Mahler and her co-authors provide a theoretical scaffolding for the understanding of child development which has potential application in a host of different areas, including the psychoanalytic treatment of adults, for which a developmental point of view is indispensable. It would be no exaggeration to say that this work represents one of the most comprehensive modifications of the psychoanalytic theory of child development since Freud published his 'Three Essays' in 1905. I would venture to suggest that Mahler's views would have been perfectly acceptable to Freud, for they are based on the most careful and essentially psycho-analytic observation of infants and children, normal and abnormal.

For Mahler, biological birth precedes the psychological birth of the infant. During the normal autistic phase of psychological development, encompassing the first few weeks of life, the infant is psychologically still in utero. Although he is an experiencing individual he does not know it, and his experiences are vague and unstructured. There is a complete absence of the awareness of a 'mothering agent'. This shades into the beginning normal symbiotic phase, in which there is 'hallucinatory or delusional somatopsychic omnipotent fusion with the representation of the mother'. In the mind of the child there is no boundary between child and mother, but both share a common boundary. In the symbiotic phase the child 'begins dimly to perceive need-satisfaction as coming from some need-satisfying part object', although this remains within the orbit of the 'omnipotent symbiotic dual unity'. It is only later that the specific 'object-bound' affect of longing occurs. The developing representations of self and mother form the organizing poles of the child's later psychological structures. Although the process of psychological labour is going on, and is perceived but not recognized by the child, psychological birth, as characterized by the twin processes of separation and individuation, has not yet taken place.

Psychanalysis–Psychiatriki–Nevrologhia. [psychoanalysis–Psychiatry–Neurology.]

By Demetrios Th. Kouretas. Athens: Scientific Editions G. K. Parisianos. 1975. Pp. 1010.

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis  

One of the disadvantages of scientists from small countries who publish in their native language is that their work remains usually unrecognized or poorly appreciated by the rest of the world. Psychiatrists and psychoanalysts (or shall I say, psychiatry and psychoanalysis?) suffer the most. Ironically, psychiatrists and in particular psychoanalysts who write in English all but ignore the contributions of colleagues in other languages, including the major ones, French, German and Spanish.

Professor Kouretas writes and teaches in Greek (do I need to say modern Greek?), the language of an illustrious but small country, and is relatively unknown to outsiders, in particular English speaking colleagues. Since he was retired from the position of Chairman of the Department of Psychiatry at the National University of Athens by an arbitrary age limit set by the Colonels' law, he is no longer the official voice of academic psychiatry, but remains still the senior representative of psychoanalysis in Greece, indeed one among a handful of analysts—three or four, actually, among the several hundred psychiatrists who practise in the country, mainly in Athens.

Trained in France during the 1920s by Laforgue, Kouretas fought hard to make psychoanalysis respectable in Greece. Except for a brief stint in Adlerian psychology during the 1930s, he remained faithful to the basic tenets of Freudian theory, taking upon himself to explain and defend it among fellow scientists, psychiatrists and, like Freud in his early years, medical colleagues of all disciplines—not an easy or grateful task.

[[RunningHead]]

Concluding Comments on the Congress Topic

Edward Weinshel

Edward Weinshel

Edward M. Weinshel
2380 Sutter Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Edward Weinshel  

Mr President, Ladies and Gentlemen. It is indeed a pleasure and an honour to have the opportunity of presenting these concluding comments on the Congress Programme and of sharing with you my observations and reactions.

It was the hope of the Programme Committee that we would have a series of dialogues in which vital issues which played an essential role in every psychoanalyst's professional life could be discussed and examined. I believe that the Committee's wishes have been fulfilled and that the Congress participants have responded to this charge quite effectively. We have read and heard various points of view on a number of these 'vital issues'; and there has been considerable clarification, if not resolution, of these different viewpoints. I have not attempted a review of the material presented in the various sessions; that is the task of the reporters (for whose help in this presentation I am most grateful) and their summaries of the panels and dialogues can be found in Congress issues of the International Journal(Shengold & McLaughlin, 1976); (Serebriany, 1976); (Curtis & Sachs, 1976); and in this issue: Dewald & Kramer; Naiman; Cramer & Flournoy; Thiel & Treurniet; Ferber & Krent). My focus is on the differences which emerged in these dialogues; and while I have tried to be an objective reporter, I fear that you will not have to strain your clinical aptitudes to expose my own inclinations and bias.

The promise of a real dialogue was already realized in the prepublished position papers of Green (1975) and Rangell (1975) and reaffirmed in the dramatic and exciting interchange between them at the Opening Session (see Shengold & McLaughlin, 1976). These are more than 'just' scientific papers. In addition to the many substantive points each contains, we can share with these respected analysts their psychoanalytic credos: a distillate of their feelings and philosophy about psychoanalysis, their clinical observations, how they think about these observations and what they do with the product of their observations and thoughts.

[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Norman Reider

Norman Reider

Norman Reider
2380 Sutter Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Norman Reider  

DEAR SIR,

Bibliography as genealogy

An idea concerning the use of a bibliography as a genealogy was fortified by an essay by J. S. Plumb (The Death of the Past. London: Pelican Books, 1973), who makes a distinction between the past and history. Each has its personal as well as social uses. For the ruling classes the past, the lists of kings, the relations to the gods, and the historical heritage legitimize authority and status. 'Nor is it merely kings or high-priests who have required the authority of an ancestral table. All aristocracies have, very sensibly, made a cult of genealogy in order to underpin their special status' (p. 27).

This idea may well be applied to the compilations of bibliographies by some writers. It is true that realistic reasons exist for the extensive lists occurring in bibliographies, an assistance in the formulation of the problem under consideration, the correct assignment of priorities, the helpfulness to future scholars who will have at hand an encyclopedic reference list, and the desire not to offend a colleague by omission of mention of his publication. But occasionally in scientific writings the valid reasons for an extensive bibliography are flavoured by additions of reference to the previous literature, the use of which give the writing an aspect of authority and legitimacy.

Psychoanalysts as writers are, of course, not exempt from the personal use of bibliographies as genealogies. A cursory overview of psychoanalytic journals shows a surprising consistency in reference to Freud, only about one paper in ten not mentioning him. Of late the percentage of papers making no reference to Freud has risen slightly. Whether this is a portent or not I have no idea. If this is a trend it seems counterbalanced by the number of references to 'Freud himself …'.

There are variants of the use of the bibliography as a personal enhancement. Occasionally a paper is published which has no references at all. But this variant goes back to Volume I of Imago, 1912. Another variant I have found only in recent literature of the last ten years or so, that of having a bibliography replete with references to papers one has written oneself. These occasionally make up the majority of the references. I offer the possibility that such a practice, justified though it may be on scientific grounds, may also partake of the personal use, a special instance of the custom mentioned by Plumb.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Family Life in Child Development

Horst-Eberhard Richter

Horst-Eberhard Richter

Horst-Eberhard Richter
Ludwigstrasse 76
Giessen 63

Horst-Eberhard Richter  

Sometimes a value judgement is implicit in the formulation of a question. And in this sense the question contained in the subject of my paper might imply the judgement that the contribution of the family to the development of the child is more important than the contribution of the child to the development of the family. Indeed, it is generally accepted as a matter of course that the family should give priority to the welfare of the child. The psychoanalyst, however, must accept the fact that in a family everyone unconsciously invokes the aid of the other members in order to further his own development. Every member of a family asks unconsciously: what do the others signify for me? Even the parents wish to develop further, indeed they have no option if they are to be equal to the tasks of the later phases of their lives. And nothing is easier for them than to exploit their children as a means of simplifying their own further development, or of freeing themselves from the pressure of hitherto unresolved problems. Even though nearly all parents subscribe consciously to the principle that they should adjust their educational behaviour to accord first and foremost with the needs of the child, we know with certainty that most of them in practice fall short of this intention. Bornstein (1934) formulated the general experience that parents act out unrecognized unconscious tendencies more readily with their children than with anyone else. And this is true not only of severely taxed, unstable, overworked parents, 'but also of healthy, clear-thinking parents who are well-intentioned towards their children; it happens not only to the unanalysed educator, but also to those who have achieved through personal analysis greater familiarity with the processes of their unconscious'.

Bornstein's conclusion cannot be seriously disputed, although many analysts seem to be attached to the assumption that the primacy of the child's welfare is as natural an aspect of parental motivation as our cultural standards claim. In other words, one turns to the parents as to purely rational, duty-bound adults, who only need to be better informed psychologically about the child and its conflicts in order to correct their educational behaviour accordingly. On similar lines is the observation that in theoretical and especially clinical psychoanalytical literature, parental behaviour, in contradiction to the patients' behaviour, is very often presented more in the sense of critical judgement than of analytic understanding. It seldom happens that in psychoanalytical case histories the parents of a patient are as sympathetically portrayed as the patient himself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dialogue on 'Freud and Female Sexuality'

Reggy Serebriany

Reggy Serebriany

Reggy Serebriany
Pacheco de Melo 2076
Buenos Aires

Reggy Serebriany  

The papers presented by Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel (this issue) and Burness Moore (this issue), as well as the discussions by Tufik Meluk (this issue) and William I. Grossman (this issue), aroused lively interest and stimulated many enriching interventions. As the Chairman of the Dialogue, Emilio Servadio, pointed out in his closing speech, the subject of female sexuality is by no means exhausted but the dialogue helped to stress the need to revise Freud's postulates on this subject, and a most appropriate way of bringing these up to date is by means of the discussion of theoretical, clinical and technical considerations.

Furthermore, the various contributions sprang from different frameworks of psychoanalytic thinking, which in turn widened the scope of the interchange. As one of the participants aptly remarked, dissimilar theoretical frameworks necessarily lead to different statements on this particular issue. The main divergences centred on the following: (1) those who base their work on the theory of two instincts (life and death instincts) and those who are ready to accept the existence of libidinal and aggressive drives only; (2) those who consider the symbolic function to develop within the first three years of life and those who regard it as an inborn function; (3) those who consider anxiety to be the outcome of a conflict within the inner world and those who consider it the result of deprivation in the outer world and of difficulties in the process of separation-individuation; (4) those who lay stress on biological factors and those who consider the psychological aspect most important and likely to weigh even with biological processes.

In view of the variety of contributions and the different lines of thought that were developed, I shall try to group the issues that came up under a number of comprehensive headings and to pinpoint, as far as possible, the participants' agreements and disagreements, while always bearing in mind that this discussion constituted one more step towards the elucidation of a subject which is still open to further scrutiny.

[[RunningHead]]

Open Forum: The Role of the Psychoanalyst in a Changing Society

Leon Ferber and Justin Krent

Justin Krent

Leon Ferber
BALTIMORE
Justin Krent
11328 Euclid Avenue
Cleveland, Ohio 44106

Justin Krent  

The forum

In keeping with the general theme— change —of the 29th International Congress, the design of the forum was itself an innovation. The Programme Committee suggested that the forum members not read prepared statements, in order to foster flexibility and freedom of discussion and to facilitate the collective participation of both the audience and the panel.

Attendance was large and the contributions of the many who spoke were discerning, moving and at times eloquent. Many aspects of the topic were explored and proposals for consideration by the International were made. A Portuguese analyst related his clinical observations and conclusions about the impact of the sudden political change in his country upon the analytic process. Two others, originally from the U.S.S.R., spoke out against the use of psychiatry by the Soviet government for political purposes. That the participation was active and that the forum moved coherently at a brisk pace can be attributed to the Chairman's skill in integrating the proceedings and to his effectiveness in ensuring those who wished to speak the opportunity to do so. He insisted in a firm yet compassionate manner that all adhere to necessary time allotments.

Preparation for the forum

Most of the panel members had met for several informal discussions prior to the actual forum. As they explored the topic, three broad areas emerged.

The first was that of the changing morality, the mores and customs in a rapidly evolving modern world, in relation to such issues as norms of sexual behaviour, patterns of drug use, plasticity of life styles, and the impact of these changes upon the ego and superego development of patients. Considered was whether such changes require the altering of both the conceptions and definitions of sexual deviation, of promiscuity, of drug misuse or abuse, and of what constitutes acting out.

Robert Wallerstein proposed a second topical area for the panel, the changing social reality, a topic which encompasses but is even broader than the concerns of the first area. Here he suggested such issues as those raised by the women's movement and the consequent altered understanding of the normal psychology and psychopathology of women, and those issues brought about by the changing position socially and legally of individuals designated as homosexuals or sexual deviants. Wallerstein (1973) had devoted his presidential address at the American Psychoanalytic Association meetings in 1972 to a consideration of the problem of reality from a psychoanalytic perspective and, as one of many examples, used the change in women's position in our society and its profound effect upon familiar notions of passive and active, of normal masochism, and of so-called feminine and masculine attributes of behaviour. The increasing number of individuals from ethnic and economically disadvantaged groups coming into therapy in community clinics has caused analysts to become involved with patients less like themselves.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Observations on the Last Painting and Suicide of Vincent Van Gogh

Marcel Heiman

Marcel Heiman

Marcel Heiman
Mount Sinai School of Medicine
Fifth Avenue and 100th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Marcel Heiman  

Christmas 1888 was an especially unhappy time for Vincent van Gogh (V.). He had looked forward so eagerly and had made such painstaking preparations for Gauguin to come to live with him in his yellow house in Arles. But after two short months, on that Christmas day, it all had come to an end with his self-mutilation. His brother Theo—instead of celebrating his engagement with the family of his fiancée, Johanna Bonger, in Holland—had rushed South from Paris to be with V. A few days later Gauguin and Theo had left—and V. felt alone again.

Only 18 months separate us from that fateful 29 July 1890 when Vincent died.

At this point I shall attempt to put together—and wherever possible interpret—some biographical data of V.'s last 18 months of life which, I believe, contribute to our understanding of his frame of mind during that time; data which I hope will help to clarify his motives for killing himself. The data are paintings and letters of that period of his life. I shall describe: (1) the painting known as La Berceuse: the 'Painted Lullaby'; (2) the 'abandoned' canvasses; and (3) V.'s last painting: his 'suicide' painting.

I. THE PAINTING KNOWN AS La Berceuse

La Berceuse (Plate I), literally 'the lullaby', is the name V. gave to the painting of Madame Roulin, the wife of the postman. It is not an easy task to trace the origin of his interest in painting La Berceuse. And yet, I think it is of importance because of the link between La Berceuse and V.'s last painting. It is in the discovery of such connexions and their meaning where psychoanalysis can make a contribution to biography.

Three persons important to V. and events connected with them have to be described: (1) Gauguin who came to Arles in October 1888 to live with V. but left him by the end of that year; (2) the courtship of Theo and his engagement to Jo, their marriage and Jo's pregnancy; and (3) the transfer of Roulin, the postman, to Marseilles.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Complementarity as a Rule in Psychological Research—Jackson, Freud and the Mind/body Problem

Henry Edelheit

Henry Edelheit

Henry Edelheit
1140 Fifth Avenue
New York N.Y. 10028

Henry Edelheit  

SUMMARY

Essentially, the principle of complementarity, as I have described it here, is a rule for the use of language. Bohr had asserted that in certain fundamental physical matters there are two truths rather than one—two views which, taken together, offer a fuller understanding of the atomic world than either was able to provide separately (Moore, 1966). Complementarity, as envisioned by Bohr, incorporated into scientific method an essential statement of the limits of scientific inquiry. Among other things, it took into account the relation of the observer to the object. It brought the subject (and the

28

subject's dependence upon language) back into the scientific world-picture, thus restoring a relation that had been tacitly set aside during the period of expanding scientific objectification (roughly, from Copernicus to Einstein). Through recognizing a fundamental dichotomy of descriptive modes (corresponding to an experiential dichotomy)7 Bohr pointed the way to a new integration of concepts in physics. An analogous synthesis can be achieved in psychology through a consistent application of complementarity to statements about the mind and statements about the brain—for these two classes of statements also correspond to an experiential dichotomy. This means that for the relationship between psychological description and neurophysiological description, the ultimate state of our knowledge is irrelevant, it will never be possible for one mode of description to be exchanged for the other. A great deal will be gained, I think, if we assume as a matter of principle that both descriptive modes will continue to be necessary, that the expectation that either mode will one day be translatable into the terms of the other is misleading and that any procedure based on such an expectation is methodologically unsound.

A very similar proposition, made by Hughlings Jackson nearly a century ago, contributed to the development of psychoanalysis as a psychological discipline, for Jackson's was a key formulation which influenced Freud to separate his 'scientific psychology' at last from the neurological language in which he had first attempted to account for the conscious and unconscious functions of the mind.

Donald Meltzer  

SUMMARY

This short paper on the psychopathology of insight and judgement has set out to demonstrate one type of disturbance which can be seen to arise from the operation of the unconscious infantile phantasy of projective identification with the internal objects, especially the mother's breast and head, experienced as the font of knowledge and wisdom. Fragments of material have been brought to illustrate the operation of the mechanism and then a more extensive description of an analysis was attempted. This latter sought to trace the relation of the patient's character pathology to a defensive structure which had been mounted in the second year of life under the pressure of disappointment and jealousy of the new baby sister. While in many ways the harshness and judgemental quality of the character was in the nature of a revenge against the parents for expelling her from a blissful confusion of identity with them, it was also a defence against ever being caught so unawares again. Thus her epistemophilic instinct and high intelligence were reenforced by defensive as well as aggressive motives. In the transference it was necessary to work through the dissolution of the narcissistic organization illustrated best in the 'bird-woman' dream. In order to do this a difficult countertransference problem of tolerating hopelessness and humiliation had to be faced, throwing light on the magnitude of the difficulties from which Mrs D.'s parents had retreated. It is difficult to see how parents, no matter how stirling, could have done otherwise.

The internal experience of these two mental acts, delusion-of-clarity-of-insight and sitting-in-judgement, seems to shade so subtly into their healthy counterparts, insight and judgement, that it is difficult to see how anything other than a widening of the field of introspection could distinguish them. Respect for the laws of evidence, attention to the quality of reasoning, soliciting the opinion of others in crucial matters and other safeguards may help. But such intellectual and social safety-measures also pay a price by throwing away the possible moment of inspiration that seems to have no evidential links, to which the laws of logic find no application and which may seem unintelligible when communicated to others for advice. And since all nascent creativity may be based on the seizing of such moments, Kierkegaard's (1941) 'leap in the dark', there comes a time when reliance on one's own introspection, forlornly, must be attempted.

[[RunningHead]]

The Changing Use of Dreams in Psychoanalytic Practice—In Search of the Dreaming Experience

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud R. Khan
Flat 7, 24 Palace Court
London W2 4HU

M. Masud Khan  

God's my life! stol'n hence, and left me asleep! I have had a most rare vision. I have had a dream—past the wit of man to say what dream it was. Man is but an ass, if he go about to expound his dream. Methought I was—there is no man can tell what. Methought I was, and methought I had—but man is but a patched fool, if he will offer to say what methought I had. The eye of man hath not heard, the ear of man hath not seen, man's hand is not able to taste, his tongue to conceive, nor his heart to report, what my dream was. I will get Peter Quince to write a ballad of this dream: it shall be called Bottom's Dream, because it hath no bottom …
SHAKESPEARE, A Midsummer-Night's Dream

Since this is my third attempt to clarify and state my work on dreams, I hope, Mr Chairman, you will bear with me if I briefly state my earlier hypotheses. The fundamental bias of my thinking is that psychoanalysis tries to abstract and make sense of the very private subjective experiences in a person. This person can be a patient, the analyst himself or a mélange of both.

In my Edinburgh Congress paper (Khan, 1962) I had postulated the concept of a 'good dream' and argued that certain intrapsychic functions and ego-capacities were necessary for a person to be able to put together a 'good dream' from his sleep experience. Prominent among these were the ego's capacity to sustain the sleep-wish, controlling excessive influx of the primary process and appropriate dosage of 'day residues' to structuralize the latent 'dream wish' into a contained dream text. I had further stated that it entailed a capacity in the ego for narcissistic gratification from the dream experience in lieu of the more primitive narcissism of the pure blank sleep-state or the concrete satisfactions from reality. The capacity to utilize symbolization and dream work was also necessary for the articulation of the dream text. I had further tried to show how Freud had based the salient features of the analytic situation on the intrapsychic conditions that prevail in sleep which are conducive to a 'good dream'. I had not given clinical material there because what was then available to me from my practice could not be exposed for professional reasons. Today, some 14 years later, I can describe the two dreams from two patients that had guided me to write that paper. The first dream is from a female patient, Mrs X., certain aspects of whose treatment I have described in an earlier paper (Khan, 1960). She had come through a very regressed and dependent phase in analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Analysis as Experience

H. Southwood

H. Southwood

H. M. Southwood
Gawler Chambers
188 North Terrace
Adelaide, South Australia

H. Southwood  

This paper follows on from two recent papers of mine (Southwood, 1973), (1974) and although there is no time here to summarize these papers adequately, I must refer a little to what I have already said in order to orientate you for what I am going to say today.

In the previous papers I made the point that human behaviour cannot be properly understood on a purely one-body, biological model. It is necessary to realize and make use of the fact that each individual is taught to communicate symbolically by his mother (usually) on a basis of mutual identifying, and that this gives him an awareness of himself and his experiencing as well as a capacity to report rather than merely to react physically to stimulation. This introduces a factor into human behaviour that does not exist with lower animals. It is still biological but more than what we usually understand by that word.

Since psychoanalysis operates by communication between two people as distinct from biological interaction, an understanding of it requires a model, a basic picture of what goes on, of what is experienced when two people talk together. By taking as a basis the process of learning to talk, and appreciating that it is the ability to talk which leads to self-awareness, awareness of others as beings like ourselves and awareness of what we call 'reality', we can see the process of analysis as an extension of the work begun by the mother when she initiates her baby into a communicative relationship.

In the present paper, then, I want to show in more detail how our analytic work can be understood as a process of bringing the patient's experiencing into the communicative relationship through mutual identifying, sharing of experience and finding words to represent what is going on.

This mutual identifying is a fact of experience sui generis and cannot be described in terms of other experiences or resolved into simpler elements. The 'here and now' feeling that one understands the other and is understood by that other is a living 'two-way' state which must be taken as a unitary, non-dissectable experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Changes in Psychoanalytic Practice and Experience

Anna Freud

Anna Freud

Anna Freud
20 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3

Anna Freud  

Members of the London Congress who read or listened to the prepublished papers by Leo Rangell (1975) and André Green (1975) had little chance to bridge the gap between these two widely divergent presentations. Rangell defended the hard-won insights of psycho-analysis and their therapeutic value by quoting their continuing efficiency when dealing with the consequences of the infantile amnesia, with anxiety, pathological defence, guilt and depression. He pleaded for adding new discoveries to the old instead of discarding the latter in favour of the former. André Green, on the other hand, deplored the failure of present-day psycho-analysis to effect cures when applied to the psychotic core of human nature or to the more severe, psychotic-type disturbances. He gave proof of experimentation with far-reaching modifications of technique and theory, offering a range of clinical examples.

It was not easy for anyone, under these circumstances, to extract from the authors' formulations what might be valid predictions for the future of the psychoanalytic discipline.

THE PSYCHOANALYST'S MALAISE

My own efforts in this direction fasten on to André Green's description of the 'malaise' felt by present-day analytic workers, a frame of mind which I cannot help to contrast with the satisfaction and excitement which governed the attitude of the pioneering, early psychoanalysts, a series of generations to which I myself belong. What caused this elated mood were several different circumstances. We felt that we were the first who had been given a key to the understanding of human behaviour and its aberrations as being determined not by overt factors but by the pressure of instinctual forces emanating from the unconscious mind; that we were the first therefore to see mental illness as a battle between rationality and irrationality; that we were the first also to possess a method, namely free association, for breaking down the barrier between consciousness and the unconscious; that this method was applicable to ourselves as well as to our patients, thus reducing the difference between the mentally ill and the mentally healthy and alter the relationship between therapist and patient from one of dominating authority to one of shared endeavour; that our new technique of enquiry and exploration of resistance and transference was identical with our technique of cure; and finally, that, apart from suggestion and hypnosis, we had no rivals in the field of mental treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Female Sexuality—A Current View

Burness Moore

Burness Moore

Burness E. Moore
150 East 73rd Street
New York N.Y. 10021

Burness Moore  

Freud was relatively modest about his contributions to the understanding of female sexuality and feminine psychology. Repeatedly he noted the incompleteness of his knowledge and the uncertainty of his findings (see Strachey, 1961, p. 244); (Fliegel, 1973, p. 389), calling attention to the need for additional information which might come from biology and chemistry, the intuitive insights of writers and women analysts, individual experience and the influence of culture. In the 70 years since the appearance of Freud's first definitive discussion of sexuality in his 'Three Essays' (1905), there have indeed been significant advances relating to the subject in other scientific fields as well as in psychoanalysis. Nevertheless, his ideas still have cogency, and their continuing relevancy to clinical data has made many of his theoretical concepts enduringly useful. Their application still constitutes the major part of our work in psychoanalysis. There are bases for dissent about others, however, which warrant an attempt at reassessment and integration of his views with new developments in ego psychology, direct child observation, biology and physiology. The recognition of this fact by the Programme Committee prompted this dialogue as well as the re-examinations that have been going on in other scientific forums.

In the Editor's Note to Freud's (1925) paper Strachey (1961) has adequately reviewed the development of Freud's theories concerning female sexuality, and I have summarized the final form of his views in an earlier publication (Moore, 1964). While it would be useful to specify just what Freud said and at what time in relation to current scientific knowledge, in order to point out the modifications he made and the many distortions of his statements by others, this would necessitate a comprehensive review of the literature beyond the scope of this paper. I shall, therefore, limit specific reference to his comments and devote uneven attention to various subjects in my discussion. Some will be barely mentioned whereas I shall expand at length on others, taking a position based on my own experience and conclusions reached from a review of the pertinent literature. In the course of my presentation, however, I shall introduce as topics for further discussion at least the major concepts I believe Freud contributed to feminine psychology. I have selected the influence of bisexuality; the prolonged nature of the pre-oedipal period and the negative Oedipus complex of the little girl; the role of penis envy in the turn to a positive oedipal complex as well as its continuing influence throughout life; the transfer of erotogenic zones; the different nature of female superego formation; and the traits of passivity, narcissism and masochism Freud associated with femininity. Many of these concepts are so interrelated that it will not be possible to discuss each of them separately. I shall, however, present certain inferences in regard to the concepts that I have drawn from the advances in ego psychology and direct child observations.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by C. Hanly and J. Masson on 'A Critical Examination of the New Narcissism'

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg
P.P.I. Michael Reese Hospital
29th Street and Ellis Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 60616

Arnold Goldberg  

When two individuals with roughly similar neurophysiological equipment view the same thing or event and each sees it differently, it is not necessarily true that one is incompetent or even wrong; rather, it may be that they each observe with a different theory. In science, theories are guides or tools to direct our observations. No one can make an observation that is devoid of a guiding theory and no theory can stand without the empirical evidence to substantiate it. But all of our theories are hunches or intuitions that seem to make the most sense at that time. They are all wrong in that, hopefully, better ones lie ahead of us. Theories merely grope towards reality and never reach it. Theories never dictate facts; rather, they help us to understand them.

I say all this today because the paper just presented (Hanly & Masson, this issue) is somewhat of a puzzle. Parts of it raise profound and challenging questions; some of its assertions seem quite simple to answer and even to refute; however, in parts the authors seem to labour under some basic and major misconception about science and the work of scientists. Let me see if I can explain what I mean by a more detailed examination of the authors' main points.

Their first point concerns the independence of narcissistic and object libidinal lines of development: a fact which they dispute. Their example of the excited five-year-old girl shows the interdependence of narcissism and other developmental lines. I cannot imagine anyone arguing this point. But interdependence does not exclude independent development. Of course there is no one part of an individual that can be considered as totally independent of all the others; yet we can easily conceptualize (but not necessarily observe) an independent development of a single part or a separate line. Perhaps the view of development as converging upon a single endpoint inhibits our remembering that development is also divergent. The case becomes a bit fuzzy in terms of the child's suffering from a lack of parental responsiveness and this being considered in turn as leading to narcissistic overestimation by way of object loss. But surely one must also see that this is a loss of a narcissistic object; of a vital relationship necessary for the maintenance of self-esteem. I confess to being puzzled at this example, which hardly disagrees with but instead seems more to support Kohut's thesis. Rather than pick at a number of errors or perhaps disagreements in this paper, I would just this once like to clarify what is certainly a major point of disagreement. The narcissistic object is not 'one from which love is demanded but to which love is not offered.' Rather, as was clearly stated in one of the articles listed in the authors' references, the definition which is Freud's and Kohut's is that of an object experienced as part of the self. The question of love is momentarily irrelevant. Nevertheless, the illustration is clear: we see a complex configuration of behaviour in any individual and our clinical theory tries to make sense of it. One cannot appeal to any higher law as to whether indeed narcissism and object love are separate lines of development. The only appeal is to broadening of our comprehensions and in this very example I think such a use of theory does so. That the child mixes aggressive and libidinal behaviour does not dictate that these cannot be studied separately and the authors do so willingly.
