[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Truth in Psychoanalysis

Ricardo Avenburg and Marcos Guiter

Marcos Guiter

Ricardo Avenburg
Paraguay 2571, 90 D.
Buenos Aires, Argentina
Marcos Guiter
BUENOS AIRES

Marcos Guiter  

In this paper we shall discuss the concepts of construction and historical truth. A new reading of Freud's 'Constructions in Analysis' (1937) shows it centred upon the question of where the truth lies and the manner in which the analyst produces in the patient the conviction of the truth of a construction. Freud begins by quoting a well-known scientist whose opinion was that an analyst treats his patient on the principle of 'Heads I win, tails you lose.' Freud rejects this view and goes on to describe the way in which analysts arrive at an assessment of the 'Yes' or 'No' of their patients. To this purpose, he makes a comparison with the reconstruction of truth in archaeology. The first sections of Freud's paper deal with this problem and the final ones with historical truth in connexion with delusions. In contrast to the attempt—even amongst analysts—to disprove delusions, to ignore the element of truth contained in them, Freud considers the possibility of recovering that fragment of truth. Finally, Freud discusses the problem of historical truth in relation to mankind as a whole. Thus, Freud begins with the assessment of the patients' 'Yes' and 'No', of their genuine expression of agreement or of denial; he then makes a comparison with the task of reconstruction in archaeology; he wonders in what way conviction is produced in the patient, refers to ultra-clear recollections, groups together neurotics and psychotics and then discusses historical truth in connexion with mankind, with the origin of myths.

There is much to be said about all these points. To begin with, the scientist quoted by Freud seems to have been partly right when he referred to the principle 'Heads I win, tails you lose, ' if we think of the tendency of some analysts to make repressive interpretations or, at least, of the many occasions when the psychoanalyst has 'his own' truth and unconsciously tries to impose it upon his patient. In this respect, perhaps because he was much more open-minded, Freud was not aware of what his patients and those who criticized other analysts did know about: ideological persuasion, the ideological pressure the analyst brings to bear on the patient. It is for this reason that we consider the scientist mentioned in Freud's paper partly right; interpretations of a repressive nature are in fact based on the tenet: the patient's 'No' is always prompted by resistance. Freud (1937, p. 262) points out that 'In some rare cases the patient's "No" turns out to be the expression of a legitimate dissent.' In the light of Freud's teachings it is our belief that the cases of legitimate dissent are not so rare as regards the question of truth.

The Nature of the Inference Process in Psychoanalytic Interpretation: A Critical Review of the Literature

Ishak Ramzy and Howard Shevrin

Howard Shevrin

Ishak Ramzy
The Menninger Foundation
Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601
Howard Shevrin
ANN ARBOR

Howard Shevrin  

From a careful study of the literature it was noted that 'Unbelievable as it may sound, in the whole vast library of psychoanalysis—clinical, theoretical, technical or applied—there are hardly any references which outline the logical guidelines or the methodological rules which the analyst follows in order to understand his patient' (Ramzy, 1974).

In what follows, a number of papers dealing with the method of arriving at psychoanalytic interpretations and not with the technique of using them will be reviewed to explain the aforementioned assertion and its effect on the rate of progress in psychoanalytic theory, practice and education.

In one of the earliest articles on the subject, Isaacs (1939) states 'The analyst is in need of clear criteria by which to test the validity of his convictions.' Indeed, this is especially important when scientific substance of the discipline is taken into account. In particular, Isaacs stresses with exemplary rigour that 'no hypothesis or conclusions can be evaluated except by reference to the methods upon which they are based' (p. 148) and that 'in the last resort differences of opinion as to fact and theory largely come down to differences of method, methods of discovery and methods of testing and verification' (p. 148).

According to Isaacs, the analyst arrives at his understanding of the analysand's behaviour through what she refers to as the 'perception of unconscious meaning' (p. 149).

One may wonder how Isaacs conceives of perception, especially those perceptions required in analysis. The analyst develops this level of perception, according to Isaacs, through 'his own analysis and his cumulative experience of other' (p. 151). Isaacs, obviously, does not have in mind a purely perceptual process as would be involved, for example, in seeing an object, for she posits that an important part is played by coming 'to understand the various mechanisms of the mental life by living through them in our patients' and indeed that '… in part this education is conceptual' (p. 151, italics ours) in so far as we 'bring to bear upon concrete material a wider knowledge drawn from general studies, such as the facts of the overt sexual life among children and adults, direct observation outside analysis of the talk and conduct of young children, and the systematized knowledge of their successive phases of development' (p. 151). It would thus appear that the perception of unconscious meaning depends on previous conceptualizations. Nothing is said about the criteria for deriving these concepts. Despite her mingling of conceptual and perceptual processes, Isaacs insists that 'our awareness of unconscious meaning in analytic work is of the nature of a perception: perception rather than inference' (p. 151, italics ours).

Dialogue on 'The Role of Family Life in Child Development'

Paul Dewald and Selma Kramer

Selma Kramer

Paul A. Dewald
4524 Forest Park Boulevard
St Louis, Mo. 63108
Selma Kramer
PHILADELPHIA

Selma Kramer  

In his introduction the Chairman, Calvin Settlage (San Francisco), hoped the Dialogue would focus on changes in society and culture, as well as changes in psychoanalytic practice and theory. He asked the participants also to consider which elements in psychoanalytic teaching and practice are established well enough to be preserved.

Settlage asked about the effects of change in composition, structure and function of the family; the impact on child development of changes in the roles of the mother and father; and variations in the societal or parental expectations of children and their behaviour. He referred to widening the scope of psychoanalysis to narcissistic and borderline personalities, as well as overt psychosis; and to psychoanalysts' studies of infants and preverbal children through reconstruction in the psychoanalytic situation and by direct observation in a variety of settings. These have led to the broader view of individual child development, of the family and the interactions of the various family members, as well as to a deeper understanding of adult developmental phases, particularly parenthood.

The first paper was given by Maurice Friend (this issue) and discussion was opened by Albert J. Solnit (New Haven). Pointing to the potential pitfall of a psychoanalyst becoming 'an environmentalist', the analyst must nevertheless be aware that the environment is constantly changing and that the changing social structure becomes reflected in the individual child through changing structures in the psyche.

Solnit emphasized that the rate of social change is increasingly rapid and accelerating, and alluded to the increased incidence of divorce, of single parent families, and of families in which both parents work out of choice or necessity, referring to Friend's illustrative case material. He compared the auxiliarly ego functions of the middle-class mother in a nuclear family with similar maternal functions for the child in conditions of extreme poverty. He emphasized the importance of the mothering figure for the young child's development of ego and superego structures and capacities—studies of severe deprivation and deficit show that such external factors and the mediation of external factors by the mothering figure may significantly influence the intensity and balance of id forces in the developing child.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Family Life in Child Development

Maurice Friend

Maurice Friend

Maurice R. Friend
262 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10024

Maurice Friend  

The simply worded topic of this Dialogue is most challenging and forces psychoanalysts to consider how much is known and how much more needs to be known. It involves consideration of (1) growth of innate personality formation of the individual child, i.e. the nature of maturation; (2) the relationship of nature and nurture—the influences of other human personalities upon the individual maturation, i.e. development; and (3) it involves, furthermore, the process of development of small group formation, i.e. the family. Considerations of the family process might be one of the applied fields of psycho-analytic inquiry.

Family life may be viewed from the standpoint of many disciplines of which psychoanalysis is only one. Anthropology, sociology, biology and general psychology have all contributed to this topic as well as the applied field of education. There is a need to reduce the 'Tower of Babel' dilemma, not by speech alone, but by sharing conceptual ideas of phenomena that are observed from multifaceted viewpoints. Presentday paradigms may be replaced by future ones that we do not envision at the moment.

Psychoanalysis, utilizing a structural meta-psychological approach, may well be proud of the evolving considerations of the child development process in the past 25 years. How can we best utilize our findings to influence family life in the field of preventive mental health? Most of us would wish to provide clinical succour to both the individual we psychoanalyse and the progeny they contribute for later family formation. Our analysing instrument creates but a small beam in the darkness of civilization.

From the vantage point of the clinical practice of child psychiatry, child analysis, and adult analysis, as well as the opportunity to teach psychoanalytic students, I am aware of the unique opportunities for variation in the life experiences and characterological developments of all individuals within their family grouping. The acute and chronic traumatic influences that both interfere with and influence the potential maturation and development of the individual personality, plus the influence of the social behaviour upon others make for many vicissitudes. To systematize this data and to evolve further hypotheses is most difficult. Thus, I hope that our Dialogue brings consideration to stimulate other analysts in the struggle to attain further knowledge. How to influence environmental change—civilization—and at the same time not to be thought of as an environmentalist is a continual task of the analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

Incestuous Failure—Studies of Transference Phenomena with Young Psychotic Patients and their Mothers

Michael Conran

Michael Conran

Michael B. Conran
Friern Hospital
Friern Barnet Road
New Southgate
London N.11

Michael Conran  

Laforgue (1936) reported an attempt to treat a schizophrenic woman psychoanalytically. He found that the analysis required the coincidental treatment of the patient's older sister—a mother substitute—to whom there was a homosexual attachment. I do not know if analysts today, treating psychotic patients, would regard this as an unnecessary departure made irrelevant by subsequent advances of theory and technique; but to an analysed psychiatrist working in a mental hospital, who exposes himself to patient–nurse–doctor and family–nurse–doctor relationships, Laforgue's response makes good sense. It is from our dilemma about the psychotic patient, as an individual to be treated and as a part of a social unit, that two apparently divergent tendencies have evolved. On the one hand therapeutic optimism is based in helping the patient to become master of himself and captain of his fate, dependent upon an enrichment, a modification and a realignment within his inner world. The alternative tendency is to regard the patient as but part of a whole family, of whose sickness he is the final expression (or signal). Modification, not to say treatment, of this unit is surely no less formidable a proposition than the individual analysis of the psychotic patient. Yet, from the therapeutic will—I do not say zeal—of both schools, we derive that accumulated knowledge without which we would not even define the problem before us.

Working in a state mental hospital (Shenley Hospital, Shenley, Radlett, Herts.) with the most disorganized psychotic patients, I found myself in considerable doubt about attempting any variant of classical psychoanalysis in a setting for which I had clinical responsibility, i.e. a ward known as Villa 21 —accommodating 20 schizophrenic lads and their nurses (Conran, 1970). It seemed appropriate that I should immerse myself in the life of this social unit in order first to observe—in a psychoanalytic way—as much of what was going on as was available to me to know, and as much as my energy and time would allow. From quite early observations I came to detect specific patterns in given patient–nurse relationships, having those characteristics to which elsewhere we give the term 'transference'1 and which have their basis in the family life of the patient. So long as I played the traditional doctor role, I could exclude myself and be excluded from the emotional content of the patient–nurse relationship. It seemed that everything conspired to invite me into reiteration of father's role in the patient's family. This can be summed up in a rhetorical question—'does the schizophrenic have a father?
